Materials_32.explanation: The accepted tolerance for modern paraglider lines is about 3 cm extension on a 6 m line under a load of 50 N (about 5 kg) - that's roughly 0.5% stretch. Modern lines are not zero-stretch (no real material is), but they are deliberately engineered to be very close to inelastic so the wing's profile and trim stay consistent.

Materials_33.explanation: Modern paraglider line cores are made of either polyethylene (brand name Dyneema, white, excellent strength-to-weight, slight stretch) or aramid (Technora/Kevlar, light brown, very dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive and UV-sensitive). Polyester is only used for the sheath; polyamide (nylon) is the wing fabric. Manufacturers typically use both core types in a mix.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the white line is Dyneema (polyethylene), the brown is aramid (Technora, Kevlar) - that's the line material mix.

Materials_34.explanation: The sheath (braided nylon or polyester) wraps the load-bearing core to shield it from UV radiation and from mechanical abrasion against other lines or terrain. A bare core would degrade in sunlight and fray on contact. The sheath does add diameter and drag, which is why some high-performance wings use unsheathed lines coated for UV protection instead.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the sheath protects the core from mechanical load and lets you colour-code the lines, but it thickens the line.

Materials_35.explanation: Even when the load-bearing core is dimensionally very stable, the polyester sheath wrapped around it shrinks over time - especially when exposed to moisture. The shrinking sheath pulls the assembled line shorter, particularly in lightly-loaded positions (C and D) where each line only carries 300-500 g and can't resist the sheath contraction.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines can shrink especially when not under load, the sheath drives that.

Materials_36.explanation: Sewing a loop into an aramid (Kevlar/Technora) line damages the rigid, kink-sensitive fibres at each needle hole - and can cut the line's strength by up to 40%. That's why splicing is used instead for aramid: the cores are intertwined without piercing the fibres, so almost no strength is lost.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: aramid (Technora, Kevlar) is dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive - you have to be careful where you install it.

Materials_37.explanation: For unsheathed aramid lines, splicing (intertwining the fibres of the core into a loop) doesn't pierce or damage the fibres, so it preserves nearly the full breaking strength. Sewing pierces the rigid fibres and can cost up to 40% of the line's strength. Fusing melts aramid (which has a high melting point) and isn't a safe loop technique.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: aramid is dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive - splicing rather than sewing.

Materials_38.explanation: When water gets into Dyneema or Aramid lines and then freezes, the ice expands inside the fibre bundle and damages the core - producing 'stress whitening' that is invisible from outside (hidden under the sheath) but seriously weakens the line. Normal manoeuvres or maximum load flying don't cause this. It's a care issue, not a flying issue: never store a wet wing where it might freeze.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the sheath protects the core - but damage inside the core stays hidden.

Materials_39.explanation: A thinner line of the same strength is essentially the same fibre core with less sheath bulk - so it has less aerodynamic drag (smaller frontal area) but is more flexible and slippery, which makes it more prone to tangling during wing preparation. That's why high-performance wings often use unsheathed thin lines but ask for careful sorting before launch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: unsheathed lines have low weight and little drag - that's why they're used.

Materials_40.explanation: About 80% of the wing's load travels through A and B risers and only 20% through C and D - so A+B carry roughly four times the load of C+D, easily more than double. This isn't an equal distribution by design: it follows directly from the fact that lift is concentrated on the front of the wing.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: 80% of the load is on the A and B group, only 20% on C and D - that's why C/D lines barely see any tension.

Materials_41.explanation: Looped attachments are simply hooked into a sewn loop on the cell wall, so a damaged line can be unhooked and replaced quickly. Sewn-to-cloth attachments require seamstress work to repair. Looped connections aren't more aerodynamic or stronger - the benefit is purely serviceability, which is a big deal for a part that gets replaced periodically.

Materials_42.explanation: Lines are matched to a specific wing model: the manufacturer chose the diameter, sheathing, splice technique and material mix to give the wing its certified flight characteristics. Substituting different lines - even if they're stronger or the same diameter - changes how loads distribute and voids the certification. Only original parts via authorised service.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the line mix is what makes the wing fly like it was newly built - you don't change it casually.

Materials_43.explanation: Different lines see different loads in normal flying - A and B carry 80%, C and D only about 20%. Lines under more load tend to creep longer, lightly-loaded lines tend to shrink. So after many flights, the original lengths drift purely from normal use. That's why periodic glider checks include line-length measurement and trim correction.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines stretch or shrink in normal use - you measure and trim 1-2 cm to bring the wing back like new.

Materials_44.explanation: A short, severe overload (line caught at launch, snagged on landing) stretches the fibres past their elastic limit. The line gets longer permanently, which shifts the geometry of that line group and subtly changes the wing's profile and flight behaviour. The line may still hold its rated load - the issue is dimensional, not strength.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines deform - get longer or shorter - which you measure during a check and correct by trimming.

Materials_45.explanation: Dyneema and aramid line cores - and the polyester sheaths - shrink over time when exposed to moisture and dirt, especially in lightly-loaded positions (C and D, where each line only carries 300-500 g). The contamination penetrates the sheath and the unloaded fibres pull tighter as they dry. Cold or disuse don't cause permanent shortening.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: Dyneema lines in the C and D area, lightly loaded, tend to shrink - moisture amplifies that.

Materials_47.explanation: Pulling the leading edge down by shortening A lines tilts the wing nose-low and reduces the angle of attack. Both the wing profile and its handling change - the wing flies faster, is easier to inflate, but is more prone to frontal collapses. A and D are opposite levers on the same AoA dial.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_48.explanation: The B lines sit in the middle of the line tree, so shortening them mostly bows the profile rather than shifting the leading edge up or down - the angle of attack barely changes. What does change is the wing's profile shape and how it handles. Not all line-group changes shift AoA; the B group is the exception.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_49.explanation: Stretching the rear (D) lines lowers the trailing edge, which tilts the wing nose-down and reduces the angle of attack. The wing's profile shape and handling change accordingly: faster flight, easier inflation, but more vulnerable to frontal collapses. Lengthening D mirrors what shortening A would do.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_50.explanation: Shortening the rear (D) lines tilts the back of the wing down, raising the leading edge - so the angle of attack increases. That changes both the wing's profile and how it handles: slower flight, harder to inflate, with parachutal-stall tendency. Any change in line-group length changes the profile.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_52.explanation: Stretching the rear (D) lines drops the trailing edge, which tilts the wing nose-down and reduces the angle of attack. The wing inflates more easily and flies faster, but with a lower AoA the frontal collapse risk goes up while parachutal-stall risk drops. D and A behave opposite to each other.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the line can lengthen or shorten, you measure it in the check and trim 1-2 cm to bring it back like new.

Materials_53.explanation: Shortening the A lines pulls the leading edge down, reducing the angle of attack. The wing inflates more easily and flies faster, but with a lower AoA it's much more vulnerable to frontal collapses - and the parachutal-stall risk drops because the wing is further from the stall side of the polar. A and D have opposite effects.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_54.explanation: Shortening the rear (D) lines tilts the back of the wing down and raises the angle of attack. The wing now resists inflating, flies slower (because the higher AoA puts it closer to the stall side of the polar), is less prone to frontal collapses but much more likely to enter a parachutal stall. D and A produce opposite effects.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

Materials_55.explanation: Lines are critical structural elements - a torn line means the wing's geometry and load distribution are compromised. Never tie lines back together, splice with random cord, or just fly without one: only an authorised replacement by an original line restores the safety margin. Ground the wing until it's properly repaired.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines hold a lot - you don't just replace them with anything.

Materials_56.explanation: Longer suspension lines put the pilot further below the wing, lowering the centre of gravity of the pilot-wing system. The longer 'pendulum arm' also means larger swinging amplitudes when the wing changes direction quickly. Short lines give crisper handling; long lines give a more pendulous, slower-feeling glider.

Materials_60.explanation: Loading a material right up to its tensile maximum permanently deforms it: fibres are stretched past their elastic limit and microscopic damage accumulates. After such an event the part has new dimensions and reduced strength compared to its original state. That's why heavily overloaded lines must be replaced, never retensioned or re-used.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in certification the wing is loaded to 8G (often more) and then checked for damage afterwards.

Materials_61.explanation: A paraglider is made entirely of flexible elements - fabric and lines. Flexible things can only carry tension (pulling) - they can't resist compression, bending or torsion in any meaningful way. That's why structural design and damage analysis for a paraglider focus only on tensile loads on lines and cloth.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

Materials_64.explanation: Lift on a wing is concentrated on the front half - about 80% of the load runs through the A and B risers, only about 20% through C and D combined. Of those, the D risers (furthest back) carry the smallest share. That's why steering by D risers if the brakes fail is risky: you're working with the lightly-loaded group that goes slack first.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: about 80% of the load is on A and B, only 20% on C and D - so C and D are lightly loaded.

Materials_65.explanation: Extreme manoeuvres impose heavy and sudden tension on the lines, cell walls and risers. Repeated near-limit loads stretch various components beyond their elastic range, so dimensions creep over time and the wing's certified flight characteristics drift. The lines won't all snap or quick-links crack - the damage is dimensional, slow and accumulates.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

video_clips.snippet: Sam: strong manoeuvres with a lot of G-load age the wing faster than gentle flying.

Materials_66.explanation: Pushing the speed bar pulls down the A risers most and the B risers less (sometimes a little C too) via a pulley system. Shortening the front risers tilts the wing forward, which reduces the angle of attack and increases airspeed without changing the profile much. The D risers are never shortened by the accelerator.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: pressing the speed bar pulls down the A risers, the angle of attack becomes smaller and the wing speeds up.

Materials_67.explanation: Pushing the speed bar in calm air shortens the A risers (and B to a lesser extent), lowering the angle of attack. The wing accelerates forward but the sink rate also rises - so on a still day the glide ratio actually gets worse, not better. The clear effect: more forward speed.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the speed bar reduces the angle of attack and the wing gets faster.

Materials_68.explanation: In a headwind the polar curve shifts and the best glide-over-ground tangent moves to a higher airspeed. Pressing the speed bar gets you to that new optimum: you sink a bit faster through the air but cover more ground per metre lost, so the glide ratio over the ground improves. This is exactly the situation accelerators were designed for.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the speed bar reduces the angle of attack so the wing becomes faster.

Materials_69.explanation: Trimmers are mechanical adjusters on the rear (D) risers. Letting them out lengthens the rear risers, lowering the trailing edge and reducing the angle of attack so the wing flies faster - same end effect as the speed bar but adjusted by hand and held in position. Tandems use trimmers because there's no foot room for a speed bar.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: tandems use trimmers on the rear risers - you adjust them by hand and they hold position.

Materials_70.explanation: Trimmers change the length of (usually) the rear risers, which shifts the angle of attack and flight characteristics. Because they actively modify wing geometry, the EN certification for a wing only covers configurations that were tested with the trimmers - if a wing wasn't certified with trimmers and you add them aftermarket, certification no longer applies. They aren't cosmetic and they aren't free of consequences.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: tandems use trimmers on the rear risers - hand-adjustable and held in position.

Materials_71.explanation: To fly faster you release (let out) the trimmers, which lengthens the rear risers. That lets the trailing edge rise - lowering the angle of attack and freeing the wing to accelerate. Tightening trimmers does the opposite (rear down, AoA up, slower). It's the same direction as the speed bar, just held mechanically.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: trimmers can be let out, which is what tandems use because there's no foot room for the speed bar.

Materials_73.explanation: Brake lines pass through small pulleys or smooth sliding rings on the rear risers so they don't scrub against the webbing. Without those guides the brake line would saw against the riser at every brake input, fraying it. Pilot habits like brake position or smoothness can't prevent that mechanical wear.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the brake line comes off the trailing edge and runs back to the handle on the rear riser.

Materials_74.explanation: Brake-line length is part of the wing's certified setup: the factory chose how much travel before reaching the stall point. Shortening or lengthening it can pre-load the trailing edge (causing parachutal stalls or losing braking authority) and voids certification. Only the manufacturer or their authorised representative can adjust it. Pilot size or harness doesn't change the brake-line baseline.

Materials_75.explanation: The brake-line length is calibrated by the manufacturer so that 'arms up' is at zero brake and the wing can use its full speed range - from full speed at the top all the way through to stall at the bottom of the travel. If brakes are too short, the wing is permanently pre-braked and you can't fly the high-speed end; if too long, you can't reach the stall to control sink.

Materials_76.explanation: With even a small brake input, the trailing edge of the wing is pulled down by the brake line itself. This lifts the rear of the canopy so the D lines aren't holding much load and visibly arc rather than pulling taut. That's the expected behaviour with brakes applied - not a sign of stretched D lines or short brakes.

Materials_77.explanation: Diagonal bracing in the harness locks the seat plate laterally. With strong bracing the pilot can't swing side to side, which both damps the feedback from the wing in turbulence and makes weight-shifting much less effective - the pilot's mass can't actually transfer to the side. Comfort and stability up, feedback and steering authority down.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_78.explanation: Diagonal cross-bracing in the harness locks the seat plate against lateral roll. Without it, the harness can swing side-to-side freely, so the pilot feels every wing movement (alive feedback in turbulence) and weight-shifting works very effectively because the pilot's mass actually transfers to one side. Cross-braced harnesses are stable but mute both.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_79.explanation: Bringing the karabiners closer together puts the pivot points near the pilot's centreline, damping lateral motion - so you feel less of the wing's micro-movements in turbulence. The downside is that closer hang points let the risers wrap around each other more easily during a collapse, so the twist risk goes up.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_80.explanation: Spreading the karabiner hang points apart gives the harness more room to swing laterally, so the pilot feels more of the wing's movement in turbulence - useful feedback for active piloting. The wider geometry also makes it geometrically harder for the risers to wrap around each other, so the twist risk drops.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_81.explanation: Karabiners mounted high up on the shoulders bring the pivot point close to the pilot, which damps lateral oscillation - so you feel less of the wing's micro-movements in turbulence. The trade-off is the high pivot makes it physically harder to lean forward and run hard during launch acceleration.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_82.explanation: Karabiners mounted low (closer to the pilot's seat) put the pivot point further from the wing, so harness oscillations are larger and the pilot feels more of the wing's motion in turbulence. The lower geometry also leaves more room to lean forward at launch, making the acceleration run easier. The trade-off: less damping than high karabiners.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_83.explanation: Adding diagonal bracing changes how the harness reacts during dynamic events - collapses, big-ear release, spirals. The braced harness damps lateral oscillations and resists twist, so the wing-pilot system behaves differently in extreme flight situations even though the wing itself, its profile and its polar curve are unchanged.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_84.explanation: Lying back in a pod-style reclined harness drops your frontal area sharply, which cuts pilot drag. Less drag means a better glide ratio - the main reason XC and competition pilots prefer this position. It's also more wind-sheltered for long flights, but it's harder to launch in and to climb into for beginners.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the prone harness gives lower drag, better glide and good wind shelter - the choice for long XC flights.

Materials_85.explanation: Lying back puts the pilot's centre of mass closer to the harness pivot line, which makes the system easier to spin about its vertical axis. In an asymmetric collapse the harness can rotate more freely and a twist becomes more likely - especially with a reactive wing. The aerodynamic glide gain comes with this handling cost.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the disadvantages of the prone harness are it's awkward to climb into and you don't have a direct view upward.

Materials_86.explanation: A back protector is passive safety - it cushions an impact you didn't prevent. It cannot replace correct launch and landing technique, weather analysis or piloting discipline. Pilots who rely on protectors as a safety net rather than as a backup take greater risks and more often end up in the kind of crash the protector was never designed to absorb.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_87.explanation: Ram-air airbags only inflate once the airflow blows into them in flight - so unpacked, they're just folded fabric and the harness is much lighter and smaller than a foam-protector version. The downside is the airbag offers no protection at launch (before it inflates), so foam wins for take-off and landing safety while airbags win for pack volume and weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: airbags only fill once we're in the air - they save weight and volume but give no protection at launch.

Materials_88.explanation: Foam back protection is already in its protective shape the moment you clip in - so it cushions an impact at launch, in the air or on landing. Airbags by contrast only inflate after the airflow blows into them in flight, leaving you unprotected during the run-up. The trade is foam's bigger pack volume and extra weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: foam protection works from the start, airbag only fills in flight - and at launch we have no protection there.

Materials_89.explanation: The accepted wing-loading range is 2.5 to 4.0 kg/m^2 for recreational pilots. At 2.0 kg/m^2 the wing is dangerously under-loaded - it will be soft, slow, easy to collapse in turbulence, and unable to develop enough internal pressure to recover cleanly. Below 2.5 is outside the certified envelope and unsafe.

Materials_90.explanation: Standard wing loading for a recreational pilot sits between 2.5 and 4 kg/m^2, so 3.5 kg/m^2 sits squarely in the normal range. Higher loading (3.5-4.2) is increasingly popular with experienced pilots because modern wings handle better when loaded up - more pressure equals more pitch authority and resistance to collapses.

Materials_91.explanation: The standard wing-loading range is 2.5-4.0 kg/m^2 with extended values up to about 4.2 for experienced pilots on modern wings. 4.5 kg/m^2 sits right at the upper limit: speed and pitch authority are great, but stall margins shrink and a collapse will be sharper. Only pilots who know the wing well should load it this high.

Materials_92.explanation: When you most need the reserve - asymmetric collapse, sudden disorientation, twisted lines - your hand has to find the release handle by feel, fast, under stress. So the single most important mounting rule is that the handle is easily seen and reached in the pilot's normal flying position. Mounting strength matters too, but no fixing point helps if you can't grab it.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_93.explanation: If the reserve attaches at the pilot's centre of gravity (same height as the pilot), the canopy doesn't automatically rotate the pilot upright after deployment - the pilot stays in whatever orientation they were in. So the pilot must actively get into the feet-down, upright landing posture before ground contact. Higher attachment points do that rotation automatically.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the priority after a reserve toss must be to land on your feet - your legs are a better protector than the harness pad.

Materials_95.explanation: Connect the reserve risers high up on the shoulder straps and behind the pilot. That geometry pulls the pilot upright once the reserve opens, putting the legs down ready to absorb the landing. Low or forward attachment points leave the pilot seated and tipped, which is the wrong body position to take the impact.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the priority is to land on your feet - the protector is never as good as your legs.

Materials_96.explanation: Reserve canopies are made of the same polyamide (nylon) ripstop as the main wing, but woven much finer and looser. The looser weave intentionally allows some stretch - that stretch absorbs the deployment shock so the lines and the pilot's body aren't slammed. Polyester or polyethylene fabrics are not used for the canopy itself.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the reserve is nylon, same cloth as the wing but woven much finer because here it's allowed to stretch.

Materials_98.explanation: Reserve canopies must be strong enough to take the shock load when they snap open - but also intentionally elastic within defined limits so that stretch absorbs the opening shock and protects the pilot's body. Pure inelasticity would mean a violent jerk; uncontrolled elasticity would mean a sluggish, slow-opening canopy. Controlled stretch is the design target.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with the reserve it's important that it's allowed to stretch - that's what absorbs the opening shock.

Materials_99.explanation: If a reserve sits packed for too long, the folds compress and the fabric layers can stick together (static cling, humidity, settling), which slows the canopy unwrapping at the critical moment. Regular repacking (typically every 4-6 months, with 24 hours of airing) restores loft and reliable opening speed. The strength or porosity of the cloth itself isn't changed by packing intervals.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_100.explanation: Reserve canopies are nylon ripstop, the same family of material that gets damaged by UV (degrades the fibre and any coating) and by moisture (which can rot fabric and slow opening on the next deployment). Cold and frequent packing don't materially shorten lifespan - in fact regular repacks are the recommended practice.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: UV radiation and moisture are the big enemies - pack the wing in the shade, dry it before storing.

Materials_101.explanation: Reserve lines are bundles of fibres surrounded by a sheath - water wicks into the bundle and is held against gravity by capillary action between fibres. The fabric canopy dries in hours once spread out, the containers in a day, but the lines can hold internal moisture for many days. Drying them through is critical, because wet lines can freeze, shrink and lose strength.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_102.explanation: The 5.5 m/s figure is measured on a test rig - reserve dropped from a bridge or helicopter with only a weight attached, no paraglider. In real life the deflated wing stays connected and interferes with the reserve canopy, making the whole system pendulum and raising the actual sink rate. You should expect a noticeably harder landing than the certification number suggests.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the 5.5 m/s and 1.5 m equivalent is measured without a paraglider - in real life it's more like a 2-3 m drop with the wing still attached.

Materials_103.explanation: The 5.5 m/s value is measured on a test rig: the reserve is dropped from a bridge or helicopter with just a weight on it, no paraglider. In actual emergencies the still-connected main wing interferes with the reserve canopy, swinging the system and raising the real sink rate. So the certified number is a best-case lab value, not what you should expect.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: reserves are tested without a paraglider - in real life the wing is still connected and the sink rate is higher.

Materials_104.explanation: The most important reserve criterion is that the canopy is big enough for the pilot's all-up weight - that's what keeps the sink rate below the certification target. Pack volume, weight and inner-container design matter for comfort but are secondary to sizing: a too-small reserve can never give a survivable descent. Always size to your real loaded weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: a reserve gets you down at about 5.5 m/s - it's about getting safely on the ground, sizing matters.

Materials_105.explanation: A reserve opens quickly if (1) the canopy fabric has low porosity so it inflates fast, (3) the pack is fresh so folds haven't compressed/stuck, (4) you're moving fast through the air to inflate it, and (5) the handle is easy to grab so reaction time is short. Double hang points may aid stability but don't speed up the opening.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

Materials_107.explanation: Of the fluids commonly found in a car boot, distilled water is the only one that's chemically inert toward the wing's coating and fibres. Petrol, antifreeze and screen wash all contain solvents or surfactants that dissolve or attack the polyurethane/silicone coating. If the wing must travel in the boot, store it away from any other liquids.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: temperature and acid are bad - a wet wing in a hot car boot is one of the worst combinations.

Materials_108.explanation: Ultraviolet radiation is the dominant source of fabric ageing in a paraglider. UV photons break the polymer chains in nylon fibres and the coatings, making them brittle and porous over time. Infrared is just heat (manageable), and X-ray/gamma exposure isn't a real-world flying issue. Minimising UV - by packing the wing in the shade between flights - dramatically extends life.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: UV radiation plays a big role - pack the wing and put it in the shade so the UV hours can be reduced.

Materials_109.explanation: Gloves should always be worn. Their main job is to protect the hands from line burn during launch, abrasion on rough terrain and the cold at altitude. Even in summer the brake lines under load can saw your fingers, and at 3000 m it's freezing even with sun on the ground. Modern paragliding gloves don't degrade brake feel meaningfully.

Materials_110.explanation: Statistically, head injuries in paragliding overwhelmingly happen during the launch run and landing - tripping, hitting the slope, low-altitude impacts. The helmet's main job is to absorb those low-energy ground impacts. Mid-air collisions with other aircraft or falls from altitude are catastrophic regardless of helmet, so the helmet is specifically designed for the take-off/landing phase.

Materials_111.explanation: Full-face (integral) helmets cover the face and chin in addition to the skull, so they protect the face and jaw during the launch and landing impacts that are most common. Modern composite full-face helmets are both light and strong. Bicycle, climbing and jet helmets give less coverage and are not optimal for paragliding's typical impact patterns.

Materials_112.explanation: An anemometer measures the speed of the air flowing past it - i.e. the wing's airspeed relative to the surrounding air mass, not its ground speed and not wind direction. It's not the same as a GPS speed reading, which gives speed over ground (and includes any wind drift).

Materials_113.explanation: The anemometer needs clean air to measure airspeed accurately. Mounting it on a riser, on the pilot's body, or up in the lines puts it in the disturbed flow around the pilot or the wing. About 1 m below the pilot's foot is far enough from the pilot's slipstream and harness drag to read the actual airflow the wing is seeing.

Materials_114.explanation: A variometer measures and reports the glider's vertical speed - climb rate in thermals, sink rate elsewhere. It does so by tracking the rate of change in atmospheric pressure with time. An audible vario is the single most useful instrument for a paraglider pilot. It does not measure horizontal speed or air density.

Materials_115.explanation: A variometer doesn't measure speed directly - it measures atmospheric pressure and reports its rate of change. Since pressure decreases steadily with altitude, a falling pressure means you're climbing and a rising pressure means you're sinking. The numerical readout in m/s is computed from that pressure trend.

Materials_117.explanation: A strengthening or approaching high pressure pushes local pressure up. An altimeter calibrated in the morning interprets the rising pressure as a descent - so by the afternoon its altitude reading is too low compared to the actual height. Always recalibrate before each flight, especially when the weather pattern is changing.

Materials_118.explanation: An altimeter derives altitude from atmospheric pressure: when local pressure drops it concludes you went higher. If the high pressure system is weakening, pressure falls during the day - so a morning-calibrated altimeter reads too high by the afternoon. Always recalibrate before each flight to avoid this drift.

Materials_119.explanation: EN certification is a European Norm scheme. It is voluntary - manufacturers aren't legally forced to certify - but in practice SHV and most national bodies expect EN compliance, and BAZL requires it for exam wings. It is per wing model (one certificate per type), not per individual airframe.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the EN certification mark is valid worldwide except in Germany where LTF still applies.

Materials_122.explanation: In Switzerland, flying with an EN-certified wing is legally required only for the official BAZL/OFAC exams. Outside of exams it's strongly recommended but not statutorily compulsory - though SHV insurance and most schools effectively require it. Across the rest of Europe EN is the de facto standard, with Germany still on the older LTF as the exception.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the EN certification mark is valid worldwide - the only exception is Germany, which is still on LTF.

Materials_123.explanation: EN certification tests a brand new wing of a specific model: the static and shock-load tests check structural strength (8G minimum, often more), and the in-flight manoeuvre sequence (24 manoeuvres scored across 90 points) classifies its flight characteristics A/B/C/D. It's a one-time, type-level certification - it says nothing about how the wing holds up after wear, age or damage.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in cert, 24 manoeuvres are flown and 90 individual points awarded - that's how you get an A or B wing.

Legislation_3.explanation: Free flight in Switzerland is governed by the Ordinance on Aircraft of Special Categories (German VLK, French OACS), which entered into force in 1994. This federal ordinance is the specific text that covers hang gliders and paragliders - it defines who may fly, what equipment is required, the insurance minimum and the marking obligation. The Confederation alone has the authority to legislate on the use of Swiss airspace.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the VLK / OACS is the Ordinance on Aircraft of Special Categories - the law that governs who may fly a paraglider and how.

Legislation_4.explanation: The OACS defines a hang glider (which legally includes paragliders) as a foot-launched aircraft that, immediately after the running start, performs gliding or soaring flights along a slope. No motor, no wheeled launch - the body and the slope are the only means of getting airborne.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: a hang glider is foot-launchable and immediately after launch is used for gliding or soaring flights.

Legislation_5.explanation: Hang gliders and paragliders are explicitly NOT entered in the Swiss aircraft register - this is one of the legal peculiarities of free flight. Every other category of aircraft has a federal registration; for paragliders the only obligation is the SHV-issued identification number affixed to the underside of the wing.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: a paraglider is NOT entered in the aircraft register - that's unusual in aviation; only an SHV identification number must be on the wing.

Legislation_6.explanation: Under the OACS no authority is required to test or certify the airworthiness of a paraglider - unlike a car there is no mandatory periodic check. Swiss free-flight law leaves a lot to personal responsibility; it is only recommended (not required) to have your wing checked roughly every two years.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: airworthiness does NOT have to be checked by any authority - in Switzerland a lot is left to personal responsibility, a 2-year check is only recommended.

Legislation_7.explanation: Because no authority tests the wing, the pilot bears the primary, first-line responsibility for the airworthiness of the equipment. Every flight begins with the pilot's own check of lines, risers, harness and reserve - and the legal consequence of any defect that should have been spotted falls on the pilot.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: airworthiness isn't checked by any authority - a lot is left to personal responsibility, meaning the pilot.

Legislation_9.explanation: Astonishingly, Swiss law only requires a helmet (and reserve parachute) for the official practical flying exam. Outside the exam they are not legally mandatory, although every responsible pilot wears them and most competition organisers require them for safety reasons.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: helmet and reserve parachute are only legally required for exam flights, although organisers usually mandate them for safety.

Legislation_10.explanation: The OACS makes only ONE type of insurance compulsory: hang-glider/paraglider third-party liability insurance. Your personal accident or health insurance covers injuries you suffer yourself, but damage you cause to others on the ground - branches falling on a car after landing, for example - is only covered by this specific paragliding liability policy.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: you must carry hang-glider liability insurance - your private liability won't cover damage you cause on the ground while flying.

Legislation_11.explanation: The legally required liability insurance covers damages caused to uninvolved third parties on the ground - the classic case being objects or people on the landing field. Damage to other aircraft in flight is NOT covered by the legal minimum, although the SHV offers an enhanced policy that does include in-flight third-party damage.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: liability insurance is what covers damage to people or objects on the ground if you cause an accident.

Legislation_12.explanation: The OACS sets the minimum cover of paraglider liability insurance at CHF 1,000,000 for third-party damages on the ground. SHV members get an enhanced policy that goes well beyond this; the federal minimum, however, is exactly one million Swiss francs.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: paragliding liability insurance is required - the SHV cooperates with a major insurer to secure good cover at low cost.

Legislation_13.explanation: Liability insurance is a cap, not a ceiling on legal responsibility. If the damages caused exceed the policy limit (CHF 1,000,000 minimum, or whatever higher cover the pilot took out), the person who caused the accident must pay the remainder out of their own pocket. That is exactly why pilots commonly take the SHV's enhanced policy.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: liability insurance is critical - it covers damage you cause on the ground.

Legislation_14.explanation: Every Swiss hang glider or paraglider must carry two sets of markings: (1) the SHV-issued identification number on the underside of the wing, and (2) the manufacturer's plaque sewn into an edge or wingtip with the basic data of the wing. Both are required by the OACS / VKZ.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: an SHV-issued identification number must be attached to the wing - always the same personal number, even on multiple wings.

Legislation_15.explanation: Article 11a VKZ requires an easily recognisable identification number (commonly written as 'max 5 digits' on the question) affixed to the underside of the load-bearing surface of the wing. The digits must be on the lower surface so that the wing can be identified from the ground in flight.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the SHV number is attached to the wing - the underside is what's seen from below.

Legislation_16.explanation: The SHV/FSVL (Swiss Hang Gliding Association) is the body mandated to manage and allocate identification numbers to pilots. In practice the number is usually identical to the pilot's SHV licence number - one personal number that follows the pilot to every wing he or she flies.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: each pilot personally receives the ID number from the SHV, and it gets attached to the wing.

Legislation_17.explanation: Article 11a VKZ fixes the height of the identification digits at 40 cm - that is large enough to be read from the ground when the wing passes overhead. The numbers are usually adhered as iron-on or sewn-on numerals to the underside of the wing.

Legislation_18.explanation: The identification number belongs to the pilot, not to the wing. A pilot who owns several wings must use the SAME number on every one of them - and that number must match the one on the liability-insurance certificate carried on every flight.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: even if you have several wings, the number on each wing must be exactly the same - your personal SHV number.

Legislation_19.explanation: The manufacturer's plaque - a small fabric label sewn into the edge or wingtip - must list four data: manufacturer name, type of wing, year of manufacture, and the minimum and maximum loads specified by the manufacturer. Serial numbers or certification class are NOT legally required on the label (although usually present).

Legislation_20.explanation: The marking obligation applies to ALL users of Swiss airspace - including foreign pilots flying here. There is no exemption: anyone launching a paraglider in Switzerland must have an identification number visible on the underside of the wing.

Legislation_21.explanation: The OACS sets the minimum age for the Swiss free-flight licence at 16. A student may start instruction at 15, but the licence itself cannot be issued earlier. There is no maximum age and no medical examination is required.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_22.explanation: On the glider chart, HX next to a CTR or TMA means the airspace has no fixed operating hours - activation is possible at any time. You must therefore assume the airspace may be live and query its status before flying (via DABS, NOTAM or radio). The other activity codes are 9 (permanently active) and temporary zones announced on the DABS.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: HX means no fixed operating hours - it could be active any time; you have to query the status.

Legislation_23.explanation: Only foreign pilots domiciled abroad who hold an equivalent foreign licence (or the international IPPI) may fly in Swiss airspace without a Swiss licence. Anyone domiciled in Switzerland - Swiss or foreign - must hold a Swiss SHV licence to fly here, even occasionally.

Legislation_24.explanation: Flight training of unqualified students is only permitted under the direct supervision of a person holding a valid flight-instructor licence recognised in Switzerland. A regular pilot's licence does not allow you to teach others to fly.

Legislation_25.explanation: Training flights leading up to the exam must be supervised directly by an instructor whose licence is valid in Switzerland - a foreign instructor without a recognised Swiss licence cannot validly supervise a Swiss exam candidate.

Legislation_26.explanation: Tandem (two-person) flights may only be performed by a pilot holding the appropriate official tandem licence. A solo pilot's licence does not allow you to carry a passenger - the tandem licence is a distinct qualification.

Legislation_27.explanation: Commercial tandem flights with passengers who have no flying knowledge of their own require a tandem 3 / Licence A. The Licence A holder may take any passenger; lower-tier tandem licences are restricted to passengers who themselves hold a pilot certificate in the same category.

Legislation_28.explanation: A Tandem 1 licence is the entry-level tandem rating: it allows flights only with passengers who themselves hold a paraglider pilot licence in the same category, as well as flight students under the supervision of an instructor. Carrying a member of the public requires a higher tandem rating.

Legislation_29.explanation: Several rules apply simultaneously after licensing: the SHV licence has unlimited validity for solo pilots, no minimum number of flights per year is required, and no medical exam is needed. All of these statements are correct - hence the 'all of the above' answer.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: no minimum number of flights is required - your brevet doesn't expire.

Legislation_30.explanation: The detailed programme of the theory and practical exams is laid down in the SHV directives, which are approved by the BAZL/OFAC (Federal Office of Civil Aviation). The Confederation thus retains oversight while delegating the day-to-day content to the federation of pilots.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the federal government authorises legislation; the BAZL delegates implementation to the SHV - including the exam regulations.

Legislation_32.explanation: Swiss Air Force operating hours are Monday to Friday, 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05 local time. The five-minute buffers at start and end are deliberate to avoid overlap with civil traffic. Outside these hours - including all weekends and public holidays - MIL OFF applies, and the alpine E ceiling rises from 3950 m to 4550 m AMSL.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: military hours are Monday-Friday 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05 - the 5-minute buffers exclude overlap.

Legislation_34.explanation: An LS-R-for-Gliders zone with the 'MA' suffix is automatically active during MIL OFF (evenings, weekends, holidays). When the military is active (MIL ON), the zone is normally closed - but you may CALL the responsible authority and request that it be opened, because the military might not actually be using that piece of airspace. The 'MO' suffix, by contrast, is active only during MIL OFF with no possibility of request.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: MA means the LSR-for-Gliders is also active when the military is not released - and you can ask for it to be opened on request.

Legislation_35.explanation: Open-air gatherings, buildings, roads, ski pistes, public transport, power lines and cables must be circumnavigated or flown over at a SAFE distance - the OACS does not prescribe a numerical minimum height. The pilot is required to maintain a sufficient safety margin using common sense.

Legislation_36.explanation: Provided you carry no goods and have the required documents (pilot licence, insurance certificate), you may enter and leave Swiss airspace freely under the OACS. Naturally the laws of the country you fly into must also be respected.

Legislation_37.explanation: Take-offs and landings on public waters require prior authorisation from the cantonal navigation administration. Like other public-property launch sites, the basic federal rule is supplemented by local consent.

Legislation_39.explanation: You must keep a horizontal distance of 5 km from the runway of any civil or military airfield without an active control zone (CTR), up to 600 m above the aerodrome. For heliports the protective cylinder is smaller: 2.5 km. The 5 km figure is the default - some sites (e.g. Trogen) have local SHV agreements that cut a wedge out of the cylinder.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: heliports get a 2.5 km radius, civil airfields a 5 km radius - and the protective cylinder only goes up to 600 m above ground.

Legislation_42.explanation: The protective cylinder around aerodromes and heliports only extends 600 m above the aerodrome reference point. Above that the airspace opens up again - so you are NOT violating the distance rule by overflying an airfield at, say, 1000 m AGL. The 5 km / 2.5 km radius and the 600 m vertical cap together form a tin-can shape.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the aerodrome protection only goes up to 600 m above the ground - above that you may overfly without violating the rule.

Legislation_43.explanation: Landing a paraglider on or beside a fixed-wing aerodrome runway is only legal with the permission of the aerodrome director (chief). Without that explicit authorisation it is forbidden by the OACS.

Legislation_44.explanation: No law requires a medical examination for the issue or renewal of a Swiss paraglider licence - neither at the start nor at any later interval. This is unusual compared to motorised aviation; the responsibility for being fit to fly lies with the pilot.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: no minimum number of flights and no medical exam are required - the brevet doesn't expire.

Legislation_45.explanation: The Swiss licence has unlimited validity for solo pilots and no minimum number of flights per year is prescribed. The OACS leaves it to the pilot to stay current; a refresher course is recommended after a long break but not legally required.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: no minimum number of flights is specified for hang-glider flights - the licence doesn't expire.

Legislation_46.explanation: For solo (Pilot) and Tandem 1/2 the Swiss licence has unlimited validity. For Tandem 3 (commercial tandem) and Flight Instructor the licence must be renewed every 3 years, reflecting the higher responsibility carried by pilots who fly passengers commercially or train others.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_47.explanation: The OACS does NOT require pilots to keep a flight logbook. However it is strongly recommended, because to begin instructor training you must later prove 200 flights - which is impossible without contemporaneous records. A logbook is also the simplest way to track the 40+ flights needed for the practical exam.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_50.explanation: The radio frequency 130.930 MHz is the one specifically reserved for air-to-air communication between hang glider and paraglider pilots in Switzerland. Use of this frequency does NOT require any special radio training. A separate frequency (123.425 MHz) is used for student training.

Legislation_51.explanation: Operating on the dedicated free-flight frequency 130.930 MHz does NOT require any special radio licence or training. Only when you intend to call air-traffic-control units (controlled airspace) would a separate aeronautical-radio qualification be relevant.

Legislation_52.explanation: ICAO stands for International Civil Aviation Organization - the UN agency that codifies the standards used worldwide, including the A-G classification of airspace classes that Switzerland uses (only C, D, E and G are applied in practice).

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: ICAO is the International Civil Aviation Organisation - they defined the seven airspace classes worldwide.

Legislation_54.explanation: AIP stands for Aeronautical Information Publication - Skyguide's master document for Swiss aviation. It is the long-form 'pilots' handbook' that contains all general, persistent information on Swiss airspace, navaids, aerodromes and procedures; it is updated monthly.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the AIP is the Aeronautical Information Publication - the general, persistent reference for users of Swiss airspace.

Legislation_55.explanation: The AIP contains the general, persistent information for all users of Swiss airspace - airspace descriptions, aerodrome procedures, navaids and similar reference material that doesn't change daily. Short-term changes go into the NOTAM and DABS instead.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the AIP contains generally applicable information for all users of Swiss airspace.

Legislation_56.explanation: GLDK / GLDC stands for Glider Chart - Skyguide's special VFR map for sailplanes and paragliders. A new edition is published every March; many pilots keep a paper copy for overview and re-check the digital version at map.geo.admin.ch before each flight.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: GLDC is the Glider Chart - we look more closely at it later.

Legislation_57.explanation: The Glider Chart is a topographical map specially produced for sailplanes and paragliders. It shows airspace classes (C/D/E/G), restricted/danger areas (LS-R/LS-D), prominent obstacles, LS-R for Gliders soaring zones, the Mittelland-Alps dividing line, military flight hours and aerodrome cylinders. It is the single most important map for free-flight planning.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the glider chart shows airspace classes, hazards, flight-restriction areas, obstacles, LSR-for-Gliders soaring zones and military hours.

Legislation_58.explanation: NOTAM stands for Notice to Airmen - a daily bulletin used in aviation worldwide that lists short-term changes: airspace restrictions, hazards on the ground, obstacles, etc. For VFR free flight the relevant subset is published as the VFR-NOTAM.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the NOTAM is a daily-published source for aviators marking airspace restrictions, hazards and obstacles on the ground.

Legislation_59.explanation: The NOTAM is the standard short-term aeronautical bulletin listing airspace restrictions, hazards and ground obstacles. (The SHV booklet's 'weekly' wording is a quirk of the question; in practice the NOTAM is published continuously and read daily before flying alongside the DABS.)

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: NOTAM - daily-published source of short-term restrictions and obstacles.

Legislation_60.explanation: DABS stands for Daily Airspace Bulletin Switzerland - the official Skyguide publication at www.skybriefing.com that gives pilots a graphical, day-by-day overview of which Swiss airspaces are active. Before reading it, ALWAYS verify the displayed date matches the day you intend to fly.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the DABS - Daily Airspace Bulletin Switzerland - is the main tool I check before going into the air.

Legislation_61.explanation: The DABS contains daily, up-to-date information on Swiss airspace: active LS-R restrictions, temporary TMA/CTR extensions (Sion and Alpnach being the only two temporaries in Switzerland), MIL ON status, parachute drops, firing ranges and similar short-term activations.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the DABS publishes daily which airspaces are currently active, including LSRs and temporary TMAs at Sion/Alpnach.

Legislation_62.explanation: IFR stands for Instrument Flight Rules - the regime used by aircraft equipped for instrument flight and flown by pilots holding the corresponding rating. Paragliders cannot fly IFR no matter how much GPS or instrumentation they carry: under Swiss law a paraglider always operates under VFR only.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: IFR is Instrument Flight Rules - only properly equipped aircraft with rated pilots; paragliders only fly VFR.

Legislation_63.explanation: VFR stands for Visual Flight Rules - the 'fly by sight' regime that applies to any aircraft operating visually. It defines the minimum visibility, the safe distance from cloud and the right-of-way rules. All paragliders fly under VFR at all times.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: VFR is Visual Flight Rules - that's the area in which we operate with the paraglider.

Legislation_64.explanation: IFR flight requires both the right equipment (instruments for blind flight, attitude reference, navigation) AND a pilot with an instrument rating. Without both, you may not file or fly IFR - which is why paragliders are confined to VFR.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: IFR is for aircraft properly equipped for instrument flight and pilots with the corresponding licence.

Legislation_66.explanation: Hang gliders and paragliders are restricted by law to VFR (Visual Flight Rules). That means visibility minima, distance from cloud, and the inability to fly into or through cloud - regardless of how sophisticated your instruments are.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: with the paraglider no matter how many instruments we strap on, we can't fly IFR - only VFR.

Legislation_67.explanation: GND on the glider chart simply stands for Ground. It is the lower limit of an airspace whose floor sits on the terrain (a CTR for example, which extends from GND upwards).

Legislation_68.explanation: AMSL stands for Altitude (or 'above') Mean Sea Level - the absolute altitude relative to global sea level (e.g. the elevation written on a summit marker). On the glider chart, AMSL numbers are shown in italic font.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: on the glider chart italic numbers mean above sea level, upright numbers mean above ground.

Legislation_69.explanation: AGL stands for Altitude Above Ground Level - your altitude measured relative to the terrain directly below you. AGL ceilings (like the 600 m AGL top of airspace G) therefore rise and fall with topography. On the glider chart AGL numbers are shown in UPRIGHT font, AMSL in italics.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: upright = above ground, italics = above sea level - the small typographic distinction has huge consequences.

Legislation_71.explanation: A Flight Level is 100 feet referenced to the standard atmosphere (1013.25 hPa). One foot is 30.48 cm, so FL 30 = 3000 ft = 914 m, which the SHV booklet rounds to 900 m. Quick mental shortcut: multiply the FL number by 30 to get metres.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: FL = 100 ft each; FL 100 is 10,000 ft = 3,048 m; from there you can derive any other flight level.

Legislation_72.explanation: FL 100 = 10,000 ft. One foot = 30.48 cm, so 10,000 ft = 3,048 m, rounded to 3050 m in the booklet. This is exactly the upper limit of airspace E over the Jura and the Mittelland - the boundary at which paragliders enter airspace C.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: FL 100 is 10,000 feet = 3,048 m, which is why airspace C starts at 3,050 m in the Jura.

Legislation_73.explanation: FL 150 = 15,000 ft = 4,572 m, rounded to 4,550 m in the booklet. This is the upper limit of airspace E in the Alps when the military is OFF (evenings, weekends, holidays) - the highest you may fly without entering airspace C.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: FL 130 is 3,950 m, FL 150 is 4,550 m - the alpine E ceilings depending on MIL ON or OFF.

Legislation_74.explanation: FL 195 = 19,500 ft = 5,944 m, rounded to 5,950 m. FL 195 is the upper limit of Swiss airways (AWY); above it sits Class A airspace where free flight is not relevant in practice.

Legislation_75.explanation: AWY stands for Airway - a controlled corridor connecting TMAs, used by long-distance instrument traffic. Swiss airways extend up to FL 195 with a defined lower limit (often a flight level) and clear lateral boundaries on the glider chart.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: AWY (airways) are the long airspace corridors that link airports and major route networks.

Legislation_76.explanation: An airway connects various airports along a defined corridor: it has clearly defined lateral and lower limits (the upper limit is generally FL 195). Inside an active AWY only IFR traffic is normally found - paragliders should avoid it.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: airways are the long airspace corridors linking airports and main traffic routes.

Legislation_77.explanation: CTR stands for Control Zone - controlled airspace (class D in Switzerland) extending from the GROUND around a runway up to a defined altitude. It encircles the aerodrome and is reserved for traffic that has a clearance and, normally, radio contact.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: a CTR always extends from the ground up to a certain altitude around the airfield.

Legislation_78.explanation: A CTR encircles the aerodrome runway, starts at the ground and extends to an upper limit defined on the glider chart. Its shape is dictated by topography and approach corridors and is rarely circular - the chart shows the precise boundary.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: CTRs are control zones around the major airports - they go from the ground up to a certain altitude; the shape depends on topography.

Legislation_79.explanation: TMA stands for Terminal Maneuvering Area (also called Terminal Control Area) - controlled airspace stacked above the CTR and used by approach and departure traffic. Its lower limit is FIXED (an altitude, not a flight level) because of terrain; its upper limit is a flight level.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the TMA are the approach corridors for take-off and landing, sitting above the CTR.

Legislation_80.explanation: A TMA is found in the wider vicinity of larger airports, stacked above the CTR in stepped boxes. Its lateral extent and lower limit are shown on the glider chart - the lower limit is fixed because the box must not cut into terrain.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: TMAs are like boxes stacked on top of each other above the CTR - in the Alps you may fly 300 m under them without clearance.

Legislation_81.explanation: A very practical special rule applies only SOUTH of the Mittelland-Alps dividing line: paragliders may fly UNDER a TMA up to 300 m above ground without clearance. This is essential in alpine valleys (e.g. Meiringen) where a TMA would otherwise box the airspace shut. It is also a frequent exam trap - the rule does NOT apply in the Jura/Mittelland.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport
Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: south of the Mittelland-Alps line you may fly out 300 m below a TMA - a tricky exam question; watch the 300 m.

Legislation_82.explanation: The ICAO defined seven airspace classes (A-G). Switzerland only uses four of them in practice: C, D, E and G. The full set worldwide includes A and B (high-altitude IFR-only/limited VFR) and F (advisory) which simply do not exist in Swiss airspace.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: ICAO defined seven airspace classes worldwide; only four apply in Switzerland - G, E, C, D.

Legislation_84.explanation: Of the seven ICAO airspace classes, only C, D, E and G are used in Switzerland - 'Ceiling Defines Every Glide' is the memory aid. E and G are uncontrolled (paragliders may use them freely); C and D are controlled and require clearance.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in Switzerland only G, E, C and D apply; G and E are free, C and D need clearance.

Legislation_85.explanation: Paragliders may use airspaces E and G without any prior permission - these are the uncontrolled spaces. C and D are controlled and require a clearance (C additionally needs constant radio contact); paragliders normally avoid them entirely.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: G and E are free - you may fly in them at any time; C and D need clearance and radio.

Legislation_86.explanation: Permanent radio contact is required in airspace C only. Airspaces E and G are uncontrolled and you fly them without any radio obligation; in D you need clearance but not strictly permanent radio. The simplest memory: only C demands continuous radio.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the C and D need clearance; in C also constant radio contact; only in E and G can we always fly freely.

Legislation_87.explanation: All aircraft - paragliders included - need clearance to enter Class C or D airspace. Class C additionally requires continuous radio contact; Class D requires clearance even without continuous radio. E and G are uncontrolled and clearance-free.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: C and D are only allowed with clearance and radio contact; only in E and G can we always fly freely.

Legislation_88.explanation: Airspace G in Switzerland extends from the ground up to 600 m AGL - a 600-metre 'carpet' laid over the entire topography. Because the upper limit is AGL, the absolute altitude of the G ceiling rises and falls with the terrain underneath.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: picture G as a carpet 600 m thick laid over all of Switzerland - above it sits E.

Legislation_89.explanation: In airspace G the VFR rules split at 300 m AGL. Below 300 m AGL: 1.5 km visibility, OUT of cloud (no numerical distance), ground always in sight, 180-deg turn always possible. Above 300 m AGL (still inside G up to 600 m): the strict 300 m vertical / 1500 m horizontal cloud distance and 5 km visibility apply.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: up to 300 m AGL you just need to be clear of cloud with 1.5 km visibility; above that the strict 300 m / 1500 m cloud distances apply.

Legislation_90.explanation: Over the Jura (and the Mittelland) the upper limit of airspace E is FL 100, i.e. 3050 m AMSL, regardless of military activity. The Jura/Mittelland-vs-Alps dividing line is drawn precisely on the glider chart; on its 'flat' side the E ceiling is always 3050 m.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in the Jura the boundary sits at 3,050 m; in the Alps at 4,550 m - quite a difference in altitude.

Legislation_91.explanation: In airspace E below 3050 m AMSL the strict VFR cloud distances apply: 5 km visibility, 1500 m horizontal distance from cloud, 300 m vertical distance. Above 3050 m AMSL visibility increases to 8 km because faster IFR traffic can emerge from cloud at high speed.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above 300 m AGL the strict cloud clearances are 300 m vertical and 1500 m horizontal; visibility 5 km below 3050 m, 8 km above.

Legislation_93.explanation: Above the Alps (south of the Mittelland-Alps dividing line) outside military operating hours, the upper limit of airspace E is FL 150 = 4550 m AMSL. When the military is ON (Mon-Fri 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05), the ceiling drops to FL 130 = 3950 m AMSL.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 4550 m only applies when the military is not active; when MIL ON it comes down to 3950 m.

Legislation_94.explanation: The location and times of LS-R-for-Gliders zones are shown on the Glider Chart (GLDK/GLDC) - which is the basic reference - and the VFR-Manual provides additional information on local procedures, daily activity codes and any temporary changes that complement the chart.

Legislation_97.explanation: At 800 m AGL we are well above 300 m AGL, so the strict cloud-clearance rules apply: 1500 m horizontal and 300 m vertical from cloud. Below 3050 m AMSL the visibility minimum is 5 km. The location (Jura) and time (Wed afternoon, MIL ON) do not change these numbers.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above 300 m AGL the strict rules apply: 300 m vertical, 1500 m horizontal, visibility 5 km below 3050 m AMSL.

Legislation_99.explanation: Above 3050 m AMSL the visibility minimum jumps to 8 km because faster IFR traffic can emerge from cloud at high speed. The cloud distances (1500 m horizontal, 300 m vertical) and the 'above 300 m AGL' threshold still apply, only the visibility number changes.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above 3050 m AMSL visibility goes to 8 km because faster IFR traffic comes out of clouds and you need more time to react.

Legislation_100.explanation: At 200 m AGL you are in the LOWER half of airspace G (below 300 m AGL) - the relaxed rules apply regardless of how high you are above sea level. Required: 1.5 km horizontal visibility, OUT of cloud at all times, ground always in sight. The 3800 m AMSL is irrelevant because the rule is keyed to AGL, not AMSL.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: up to 300 m AGL: 1.5 km visibility, clear of cloud, ground in sight, 180-deg turn possible.

Legislation_101.explanation: The exact location of the dividing line between the Jura/Swiss Plateau and the Alps is printed precisely on the Glider Chart (GLDK/GLDC). It is also described in the AIP, but the chart is the operational reference the pilot uses pre-flight.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the dividing line runs from Montreux across central Switzerland to the Alpstein and on to Austria - precisely drawn on the chart.

Legislation_102.explanation: At 500 m AGL you are above 300 m AGL but still in airspace G (because G goes up to 600 m AGL). The strict 1500 m horizontal and 300 m vertical cloud distances apply, but the visibility is only 1.5 km because the 5 km visibility only kicks in once you are in airspace E (i.e. above 600 m AGL) below 3050 m AMSL.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 300 m AGL is the dividing line - upper half of G demands the large cloud clearances but only 1.5 km visibility while still in G.

Legislation_103.explanation: An activated LS-R is a defined area with special rules. Red LS-Rs (the standard kind, restricted areas) are forbidden when active - no entry. Green LS-Rs (LS-R for Gliders, the soaring zones) are open to paragliders/sailplanes with REDUCED cloud distances of 50 m vertical and 100 m horizontal.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: red Rs mean restricted, no flying; LS-D is danger - legally allowed but not recommended.

Legislation_104.explanation: Military operating hours are printed on the Glider Chart (GLDK/GLDC) - the top of the chart shows the standard schedule (Mon-Fri 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05). Activation on a given day is confirmed by 'MIL ON' on the chart and the DABS.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: military hours are Monday to Friday, 07:35-12:05 and 13:15-17:05, printed on the glider chart.

Legislation_105.explanation: Inside an active LS-R for Gliders the cloud distances are minimised to 100 m horizontal and 50 m vertical. Above 3050 m AMSL the visibility minimum is 8 km (because of fast IFR traffic). 900 m AGL is well above 300 m AGL so we are clearly in the 'upper-half' regime, only with the reduced cloud distances.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in the glider zone the cloud distance is minimised: 50 m vertical and 100 m horizontal - the visibility numbers stay the same.

Legislation_106.explanation: Inside an active LS-R for Gliders the cloud distances are minimised to 100 m horizontal and 50 m vertical. Below 3050 m AMSL the visibility minimum is 5 km (the 8 km only kicks in above 3050 m). At 900 m AGL we are in the 'above 300 m AGL' regime, just with reduced cloud distances.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: inside the glider zone: 50 m / 100 m cloud clearances; the visibility minima stay unchanged.

Legislation_107.explanation: Below 300 m AGL you are in the LOWER half of airspace G - and the LSR-for-Gliders cloud-clearance rule applies only ABOVE 300 m AGL. So even inside the chart footprint of an LSR-for-Gliders box, at 200 m AGL the standard G-lower rules apply: 1.5 km visibility, clear of cloud, ground in sight.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: up to 300 m AGL the relaxed rule applies - 1.5 km visibility, clear of cloud, ground in sight.

Legislation_108.explanation: An LS-R for Gliders is a defined airspace, active from 1 March to 31 October, where paragliders and sailplanes enjoy REDUCED minimum cloud distances (50 m vertical, 100 m horizontal) compared to the standard 300 m / 1500 m. Outside that season the zone is not in force - a frequent exam trap in winter.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: LS-Rs for Gliders are active from 1 March to 31 October; outside that they don't apply - an exam trap.

Legislation_109.explanation: An active LS-D (danger area) is a defined airspace where dangerous activities for aircraft may be taking place on the ground - typically live firing or military exercises. Entry is NOT strictly forbidden (unlike red LS-Rs), but is discouraged for obvious safety reasons.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: LS-D is Danger - you can in principle fly in, but it's not recommended because shooting exercises may be taking place below.

Legislation_110.explanation: Switzerland recognises three slope-glider exam categories: paraglider, delta (hang glider) and rigid wing. A pilot may add categories by passing the relevant theory subjects and the practical exam for the new category.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_111.explanation: The SHV/FSVL is the federation mandated by the BAZL/OFAC to issue the official licences to slope-glider pilots. Although the OFAC retains ultimate authority, the practical issuing of licences is delegated to the SHV.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the FOCA delegates implementation of all hang-glider provisions to the SHV, which issues licences.

Legislation_112.explanation: The maximum allowable interval between passing the theory exam and the practical exam is 36 months. Beyond that the theory has to be re-sat - a hard limit set by SHV directives approved by the BAZL.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_114.explanation: If a candidate believes the examiner's decision was not made in accordance with the rules, the appeal goes to the BAZL first; an unsuccessful appeal there may then be carried to the Swiss Federal Administrative Court (Bundesverwaltungsgericht), which is the answer in the SHV booklet.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_115.explanation: An appeal against the examiner's decision must be filed within 30 days. The deadline is fixed in the SHV directives and runs from the day the examiner's decision is communicated to the candidate.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_117.explanation: Before the practical exam a paraglider candidate must have at least 50 high (major) flights verified by a Swiss flight instructor; the minimum is 30 for delta (hang glider) and 20 for rigid wing. These reflect the relative difficulty of each class.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_118.explanation: The required high flights must be spread over a minimum number of different sites: 5 sites for paragliders, 3 for deltas, 3 for rigid wings. The site where the practical exam itself takes place does not count toward these minimums.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_119.explanation: To sit the practical exam your wing must be recognised by the SHV as airworthy (with an exception for rigid wings) and bear all the legally required markings - identification number and manufacturer's plaque. An uncertified school wing or a wing without identification cannot be used in the exam.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_120.explanation: The practical exam consists of two flight tasks: a precision landing with two consecutive 360-deg circles to the right (in max 20 s), and a precision landing with a figure-eight (left then right, max 30 s). Three flights are allowed for the two tasks combined.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_121.explanation: For the precision landing, paragliders must land in a target circle 30 m in diameter; deltas and rigid wings get 80 m. The larger circle for deltas/rigid reflects the much higher landing speed and smaller flare margin of these aircraft.

guide_chapter.title: Examination and Licensing of Free Flight Pilots

Legislation_123.explanation: Right-of-way is ranked by manoeuvrability: the LESS manoeuvrable aircraft has priority. Paragliders/hang gliders/sailplanes rank above airships, and airships rank above military jets/helicopters because jets are the most manoeuvrable of all. Top of the ladder: aircraft in distress, then balloons.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the priority ladder - in distress first, then balloons, then PG/HG/sailplanes, then airships, towing aircraft, finally powered aircraft and helicopters.

Legislation_125.explanation: Paragliders, hang gliders and sailplanes share the same priority class (all three are 'unpowered slope/soaring aircraft') and rank ABOVE tow planes, which are powered and more manoeuvrable. The motto: less manoeuvrable = higher priority.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: paragliders and sailplanes are in the same priority category; then come zeppelins and towing motorised aircraft.

Legislation_126.explanation: Balloons sit second on the priority ladder (only behind aircraft in distress); paragliders/hang gliders come next; dirigibles (airships/zeppelins) are below the soaring class. The order reflects manoeuvrability - balloons can barely steer, while a dirigible has engines and rudder.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: balloons rank second after an aircraft in distress; paragliders/sailplanes rank above zeppelins/airships.

Legislation_127.explanation: The OACS sets no numerical minimum distance to other aircraft - the requirement is simply 'sufficient distance to avoid any risk of collision'. Use common sense: stay well clear, especially of balloons and large dirigibles, because they cannot evade you.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the distance isn't numerically defined - just 'sufficient safety distance'. Better a bit more than a bit less.
