Legislation_128.explanation: When flight paths cross at the same altitude, the paraglider has priority over a powered aeroplane: the more manoeuvrable aircraft (the powered one) must give way to the less manoeuvrable (the paraglider). The rule is built around manoeuvrability, not size or altitude.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: powered aircraft and helicopters - the most manoeuvrable - sit at the bottom of the ladder; they have to give way.

Legislation_129.explanation: When two aircraft of the same priority class meet head-on in free airspace, BOTH turn right. This is the universal aviation rule, mirroring road traffic in many countries, and it removes any negotiation between the pilots.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: when flying head-on we evade to the right; not only at 90 degrees but always head-on, evade to the right.

Legislation_130.explanation: On a slope, head-on encounters are resolved by manoeuvrability: the pilot with the slope on the LEFT has room to escape (turn right means turning away from the ridge) and must give way; the pilot with the slope on the RIGHT has no room to evade safely and keeps course.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: on the slope, head-on you evade to the right - and one wing has no options to the right, so the other gives way early.

Legislation_132.explanation: Same rule as for two paragliders meeting head-on: BOTH aircraft turn right. The fact that one is a faster sailplane does not change the priority - same class of priority, same evasive rule.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: head-on always evade to the right.

Legislation_133.explanation: Overtaking another paraglider is allowed in free airspace - to the right, with the overtaken pilot keeping right of way - but is FORBIDDEN next to a slope, where the squeezed pilot has nowhere safe to go. The slope concentrates lift, traffic and terrain, so the rules are stricter.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: overtaking in free airspace is allowed; on the slope it's forbidden.

Legislation_134.explanation: Overtaking in free airspace is done to the RIGHT, and the glider being overtaken always has right of way - so you must give them room and absolutely never force the manoeuvre. Overtaking is defined as approaching from within the rear 140-deg arc (i.e. behind the 70-deg-from-tail line on either side).

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the one being overtaken always has right-of-way; overtaking is defined by the 140-degree rear arc.

Legislation_137.explanation: There is no numerical minimum distance to other gliders - the rule is simply that the distance must be such that there is no risk of collision. Stay well clear, anticipate their flight path and turn away early; better excessive caution than a near miss.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: distance isn't numerically defined - sufficient safety distance; better a bit more than too little.

Legislation_138.explanation: Vertical separation is permitted - you may fly above or below another paraglider provided you keep a sufficient safety distance. Be especially aware of wingtip vortices and the lower glider's right of way in landing approach.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: distance is just defined as sufficient safety distance - common sense applies.

Legislation_140.explanation: On landing approach, the LOWER glider has right of way in all situations. The reasoning is practical: the lower pilot is the one closer to the ground and has fewer options to extend the approach, so the higher pilot must adapt.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

Legislation_141.explanation: The OACS does NOT prescribe a minimum distance to a slope - it is left to the pilot's judgement. The relevant rule sets are the right-of-way rules on the slope (slope-on-left gives way) and the prohibition of overtaking near a slope, not a numerical clearance figure.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

Legislation_142.explanation: A yellow cross laid out in the middle of a landing field means landing on this field is TEMPORARILY prohibited - typically because of an event on the ground, a competition or temporary works. Pick an alternate landing site.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

Legislation_144.explanation: Whether the Swiss SHV licence is accepted abroad depends on the country - many states accept it (especially neighbouring countries with reciprocal recognition), but it is the pilot's responsibility to check and respect the local laws and procedures of the destination country.

Legislation_145.explanation: Friday 14:00 in November is MIL ON (Mon-Fri 13:15-17:05). LS-R3 active doesn't affect the X here. In the Jura/Mittelland the E-top sits at 3050 m AMSL regardless of military activity. At 800 m AGL we are well above 300 m AGL, so the strict cloud rule applies: 300 m vertical, 1500 m horizontal, 5 km visibility (below 3050 m).

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above 300 m AGL the strict cloud distances apply: 300 m vertical, 1500 m horizontal, 5 km visibility below 3050 m.

Legislation_146.explanation: At 200 m AGL you are in the lower half of airspace G (below 300 m AGL), where the relaxed VFR rules apply: 1.5 km horizontal visibility, OUT of cloud at all times, ground always in sight, and a 180-deg turn must always be possible. Day, time and season don't change this.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: up to 300 m AGL: 1.5 km visibility, clear of cloud, ground in sight, 180-deg turn possible.

Legislation_147.explanation: The X is inside a restricted zone (aerodrome cylinder, CTR or active LS-R) on a Tuesday at 09:00 - during military hours and weekday business. Without special permission flying is prohibited at this location. Knowing where you are on the chart and which zones are active is the precondition for a legal flight.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: ask yourself the five questions at the launch site - season, time, side of dividing line, altitude, location relative to restricted zones.

Legislation_148.explanation: Saturday is MIL OFF and March 1st-onwards is inside the LSR-for-Gliders season. The 2750 m AMSL ceiling here is the upper limit of an LSR-for-Gliders box at this point. Above 300 m AGL (we are at 900 m AGL) the strict cloud distances apply (300 m / 1500 m, 5 km visibility below 3050 m) unless you are INSIDE the active glider zone with reduced minima - the answer takes the strict numbers because the question gives the upper limit of the LSR-for-Gliders as the cap.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: as soon as you climb above the LSR-for-Gliders upper limit, the large cloud distances apply again - a classic exam trap.

Legislation_150.explanation: In the Jura/Mittelland the airspace E ceiling is 3050 m AMSL regardless of MIL status. North of the dividing line a TMA has a sharp lower limit (here 1350 m AMSL) - paragliders may NOT fly under it in the Jura (the 300-m-AGL underflight rule applies only south of the dividing line). At 2000 m AMSL you are between 1350 and 3050 m, so the strict cloud distances apply: 5 km visibility, 1500 m / 300 m clearances.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the 300-m-below-TMA rule applies ONLY south of the Mittelland-Alps line; in the Jura the chart limit is sharp.

Legislation_152.explanation: Monday 15:00 is MIL ON (Mon-Fri 13:15-17:05). LS-R2 active is a restricted area but the X is not inside it. In the Jura/Mittelland the E ceiling stays at 3050 m AMSL regardless of MIL. At 2500 m AMSL (well above 300 m AGL) the strict cloud distances apply: 5 km visibility (below 3050 m), 1500 m horizontal, 300 m vertical.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above 300 m AGL the strict cloud distances apply: 5 km visibility below 3050, 1500 m horizontal, 300 m vertical.

Legislation_153.explanation: Sunday is MIL OFF and August is inside the LSR-for-Gliders season. At 2700 m AMSL we are above the LSR-for-Gliders upper limit (2450 m at that location) - so even though the chart marks it as a glider zone, you are ABOVE its top: the LARGE cloud clearances (300 m / 1500 m) and 5 km visibility (below 3050 m AMSL) apply. In the Mittelland/Jura the E ceiling sits at 3050 m AMSL.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan walks through the Sunday 14:00 August exam example: you are above the LSR-for-Gliders 2450 m top, so large cloud distances apply.

Legislation_154.explanation: Thursday at 10:00 is MIL ON (Mon-Fri 07:35-12:05). The X is inside a permanently or daily active restricted/controlled zone (an aerodrome cylinder, CTR or active LS-R) at 1500 m AMSL - without special permission flying there is prohibited. Always run the five pre-flight questions: season, MIL status, dividing-line side, altitude, location vs restricted zones.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the five pre-flight questions - season, time/military, side of dividing line, altitude, restricted-zone location.

Legislation_155.explanation: Sunday is MIL OFF, so in the Alps airspace E goes up to 4550 m AMSL. At 4900 m AMSL with only 900 m of ground clearance you are well into airspace C - and the 600 m AGL G ceiling above the peak does NOT extend to 900 m. Without special permission flying is therefore prohibited.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above a 4200 m peak you may legally climb to 4800 m - 600 m AGL applies on top of every peak.

Legislation_156.explanation: Monday 15:00 is MIL ON (Mon-Fri 13:15-17:05); in the Alps the E ceiling therefore comes down from 4550 to 3950 m AMSL. LS-D14 active is a DANGER area (live firing below 2200 m AMSL) - it is not legally forbidden to fly in it, but flying below 2200 m AMSL is life-threatening. The cloud distances are the standard 300 m / 1500 m with 5 km visibility (below 3050 m).

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: LS-D is Danger - not legally forbidden, but flying through firing exercises is not recommended.

Legislation_157.explanation: This is the classic Meiringen example: south of the dividing line, you may fly under a TMA up to 300 m above ground without clearance. At 150 m AGL you are in the lower half of airspace G - the relaxed VFR rule applies: 1.5 km horizontal visibility, clear of cloud, ground always in sight. Ceiling: 300 m AGL (the TMA's underflight rule), NOT 600 m, because the TMA above you is active.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan walks through the Meiringen example: south of the line, 150 m AGL is legal under a TMA up to 300 m AGL.

Legislation_158.explanation: Saturday is MIL OFF, so in the Alps the E ceiling is 4550 m AMSL. February is OUTSIDE the LS-R-for-Gliders season (1 Mar-31 Oct), so glider-zone reduced clearances don't apply. LS-R11 inactive: no restriction. At 4100 m AMSL (above 3050 m) visibility must be 8 km, with 300 m / 1500 m cloud distances. The 1600 m of ground clearance means you are in airspace E, not G.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: LS-Rs for Gliders are active only from 1 March to 31 October - in February they don't apply.

Legislation_162.explanation: The tag on the chart shows a lower limit of 2050 m AMSL (italic = above sea level) and an upper limit of 3950 m at standard atmosphere (flight level in blue highlight). 3950 m AMSL is FL 130 - the alpine E ceiling during MIL ON. The 2050 m floor is a fixed AMSL altitude (italics) and does not fluctuate with pressure.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: italic = above sea level, upright = above ground; blue background = flight level (standard atmosphere).

Legislation_163.explanation: On a Sunday (MIL OFF) in June (inside LSR-for-Gliders season 1 Mar-31 Oct) the marked LSR-for-Gliders box is active. Its lower limit is given as 600 m AGL (upright = above ground), its upper as 3950 m at standard atmosphere (FL 130, blue background). Inside it the reduced cloud clearances of 50 m / 100 m and the standard visibility minima apply.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: LSR for Gliders are open from 1 March to 31 October - on a Sunday in June they are active.

Legislation_164.explanation: The marking shows a TMA south of the dividing line whose lower limit allows paragliders to fly underneath up to 300 m AGL without clearance/radio. Above 300 m AGL you enter the controlled airspace and need radio communication (and clearance). Below 300 m AGL you are legal in G without any radio.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: south of the dividing line you may fly 300 m below the TMA without clearance - above that you need radio.

Legislation_165.explanation: The chart symbol shown - a 5 km cylinder with the specific mixed civil/military marking - represents a combined civil and military aerodrome. The 5 km radius applies (the standard for an airfield runway up to 600 m above its reference point), and the field is used by both traffic types.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: heliport with 2.5 km, civil airfield, mixed military/civil airfield, and purely military airfields circled in red.

Legislation_166.explanation: TMA Emmen (LSME) has the activity code HX - 'no fixed hours, must be queried' - so it is on the chart as a permanent box that may activate at any time. TMA Alpnach (LSMA) is a TEMPORARY TMA, activated only via NOTAM/DABS on the day; Sion and Alpnach are the only two such temporary TMAs in Switzerland, hence the visually finer border.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: Sion and Alpnach are the only temporary TMAs in Switzerland, communicated via the DABS - the chart border is a bit finer.

Legislation_167.explanation: Sunday is MIL OFF, so in the Alps the E ceiling is 4550 m AMSL (= FL 150 / QNE). However, the 600 m AGL G ceiling overrides locally: above a peak you may climb to 600 m above that peak even if it exceeds 4550 m AMSL. The rule is the HIGHER of the two values.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: above a 4200 m peak you may legally climb to 4800 m - 600 m AGL applies on top of every peak.

Legislation_168.explanation: Monday at 14:00 is MIL ON (Mon-Fri 13:15-17:05); in the Alps the E ceiling drops from 4550 to 3950 m AMSL (= FL 130 / QNE). The 600 m AGL G ceiling still overrides locally above a peak. The rule is again the HIGHER of the two values - whichever permits more altitude at that point.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: when MIL ON, the alpine boundary drops to 3950 m; the 600 m AGL rule above peaks still applies.

Legislation_169.explanation: South of the Mittelland-Alps dividing line, paragliders may fly under a TMA up to 300 m above ground without clearance. That is the practical minimum lower limit for a paraglider in the Alps: regardless of what the chart shows as the TMA lower altitude, the rule allows you 300 m AGL underflight.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: south of the dividing line, you may fly out 300 m below the TMA - but only in the Alps.

Legislation_170.explanation: North of the dividing line (Jura/Mittelland) the underflight rule does NOT apply. A paraglider may only ascend to the lower limit indicated on the chart - whatever the chart shows is the hard ceiling. The 300-m-below rule is an alpine-only relaxation.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport
Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the 300 m below TMA is ONLY south of the Mittelland-Alps line; in the Jura the chart limit applies.

Legislation_171.explanation: The 3050 m Jura E-ceiling is a flight level (FL 100) - it follows the standard atmosphere of 1013.25 hPa. With a high-pressure QNH of 1035 hPa (above standard) the pressure surface FL 100 sits about (1035-1013) x ~8 m/hPa ~ 200 m HIGHER than standard - so you may legally climb slightly above 3050 m AMSL. The trick: boundaries given as flight levels move with the weather.

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: at 1037 hPa ground pressure the upper limit shifts about +200 m; in a deep low it's -200 m.

Legislation_172.explanation: With a low-pressure QNH of 1005 hPa (below standard 1013.25) the FL 100 pressure surface sits about 60-80 m LOWER than standard, so the Jura E ceiling effectively comes down to slightly less than 3050 m AMSL. Flight-level-based boundaries shift down in a low and up in a high.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in a deep low the flight level boundary makes for minus 200 m - flight-level limits move with the weather.

Legislation_173.explanation: North of the Mittelland-Alps dividing line, the lower limit of a TMA is HARD and matches the value printed on the chart. There is no underflight relaxation here - you cannot legally climb closer than the printed lower limit to the TMA.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the 300 m-below-TMA rule applies only south of the dividing line; north of it the chart limit is sharp.

Legislation_174.explanation: FIZ stands for Flight Information Zone - an airspace within which a Flight Information Service is provided (information and alerting, but no clearance). It is distinct from RMZ (Radio Mandatory Zone) and TMZ (Transponder Mandatory Zone), all three of which feature on Swiss charts.

Legislation_176.explanation: When the tag shows the lower limit 'in accordance with NOTAM/DABS' the box is one of the temporary TMAs (Sion or Alpnach), activated daily. The upper limit 3950 m at standard atmosphere is FL 130 (highlighted in blue). On a day the bulletin marks it as inactive you ignore the box; when active you must avoid it.

diagrams.title: CTR, TMA and AWY around an airport

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: Sion and Alpnach are the only TMAs activated via the DABS - the chart border is a bit finer to distinguish them.

Legislation_177.explanation: The Jura E-top is FL 100 (referenced to 1013.25 hPa). With QNH 1037 hPa the surface shifts up by approximately (1037-1013) x 8 m/hPa ~ 200 m, putting the actual E ceiling at roughly 3250 m AMSL. Flight-level-based boundaries move with the weather; in a strong high they rise.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: at 1037 hPa ground pressure the upper limit can be about +200 m; this is exactly what the exam example asks about.

Legislation_178.explanation: February is OUTSIDE the LSR-for-Gliders season, so no glider-zone relaxations apply. Sunday is MIL OFF (but in the Jura that's irrelevant; ceiling is always 3050 m). At 500 m AGL we are above 300 m AGL (so strict cloud distances 300 m / 1500 m apply) but still in airspace G (G goes up to 600 m AGL), so visibility is still 1.5 km - the 5 km only kicks in above G.

workbook_chapter.title: VFR rules and right of way

diagrams.title: Swiss airspace cross-section: Mittelland vs. Alps

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 300 m AGL is the dividing line - upper half of G needs the large cloud clearances but still only 1.5 km visibility.

Legislation_179.explanation: In a Radio Mandatory Zone (RMZ) entry is only permitted with continuous radio monitoring on the published frequency AND so-called 'blind transmissions' - call-outs by which you announce your position even if no ATC unit responds. It is not controlled airspace per se, but compulsory listening watch is the rule.

Practical Flying_2.explanation: When you fly with a head cold or middle-ear infection, the Eustachian tubes are swollen and can no longer equalise pressure on the way down. As the outside pressure rises, the trapped air in the inner ear is compressed, the eardrum is pushed inward and stretches violently - producing sudden, very sharp pain (and risk of barotrauma) rather than slow performance loss. The textbook reasons not to fly that day.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the very first thing is whether you're even fit to fly - clear-headed, not hungover, no medication that could mask pain, no condition that flying could worsen.

Practical Flying_3.explanation: Up to about 4000 m above sea level a resting body still adapts well to the falling oxygen partial pressure - hyperventilation and faster pulse compensate enough that you keep functioning. Higher up, those mechanisms run out and acute mountain sickness or hypoxia set in. Paragliding without oxygen above this band is risky for anyone not specifically acclimatised.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

Practical Flying_6.explanation: Hypoxia tolerance is genuinely improved only by raising the body's aerobic baseline and by spending time at altitude so the blood/lung response adapts. Regular endurance training and a stepwise acclimatisation week (sleep low, climb high) are the two interventions with measurable effect; deep breaths, vitamin pills and motion-sickness drugs do not change how much O2 the blood can carry.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

Practical Flying_7.explanation: The standard wind-chill table for paragliding tabulates exactly this scenario: ambient air 2 deg C and an airspeed of about 32 km/h feels like -11 deg C on exposed skin. The 13-degree drop comes from forced convection stripping the warmed boundary layer off your face and hands - and is why a hard-shell collar, full-finger gloves and a hot drink before launch matter even on a mild spring day.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

Practical Flying_8.explanation: A cold body has to spend a much larger fraction of its energy budget on shivering and on maintaining core temperature, so muscle output drops while basal oxygen demand actually rises. The net effect for the pilot: slower reactions, weaker grip on the brakes and a much smaller decision window - which is exactly when accidents happen.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

Practical Flying_9.explanation: Constant airflow over the body, mouth and nose accelerates evaporation from skin and respiratory tract, so fluid loss climbs sharply during a long flight. The result of skipping fluids is a headache, sluggish thinking and slower reflexes hours into the flight - that's why Sam stresses 'drink, drink, drink' in the pre-flight checklist even though it means using a urinal condom or similar in the air.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

video_clips.snippet: Sam: drinking matters most - skipping it because of the toilet problem gives you a headache, slows you down and ruins the flying day; bring a solution and drink throughout.

Practical Flying_10.explanation: QNH is the local sea-level pressure that aerodromes broadcast after correcting the station reading down to mean sea level using the ICAO standard atmosphere. With this number dialled into the altimeter it then reads altitude above sea level rather than above the station. So 'Zurich QNH 1015' is purely a calibration value, not the maximum or standard pressure for that day.

Practical Flying_11.explanation: Both stations report QNH at sea level, so the 8 hPa drop from Zurich (1015) to Lugano (1007) is a real north-south pressure gradient across the Alps. Wind always blows from high to low pressure, so this configuration drives air over the main Alpine crest from north to south - the classic north-foehn pattern with strong, gusty winds on the south side.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the FFG rule covers foehn, front and thunderstorm - if any of these turn up in the forecast, ears get big and we check carefully.

Practical Flying_13.explanation: The 4 hPa rule of thumb between Zurich (north of the Alps) and Lugano (south) is the practical foehn threshold used by Swiss pilots. Below that the pressure gradient is too weak to drive the cross-Alpine flow, above it the air is forced over or around the ridge and you get the characteristic strong, gusty, dry winds on the lee side. Always check the QNH pair before flying.

Practical Flying_15.explanation: When the route leaves familiar Alpine terrain for the flatlands you need the document that shows controlled airspace, prohibited zones, glider sectors and especially the high-tension power lines that criss-cross the lowlands - the glider/gliding map (Segelflugkarte). DABS and NOTAM cover only short-term restrictions; the VFR Manual is reference material rather than a navigation chart.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the glider chart describes the legal situation, and you also have to check the DABS each flying day for active airspace restrictions.

Practical Flying_17.explanation: Good preparation always covers the same four pillars: match your plan to the actual conditions, walk the landing field, identify all cables and obstacles, and respect wildlife sanctuaries. There is no requirement to register a flight or notify an airfield chief - those distractor options exist only to pad the question.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: at the site you inspect the landing field first - cables, big trees, where Plan B is - because down low you have very little altitude to react.

Practical Flying_18.explanation: When you fly into an unfamiliar landing field, the very first questions are: in which wind directions can I approach safely, and which obstacles must I clear on each approach? Doctor surgeries and pack-up spots are operational details for after landing, not life-and-death information you need while still in the air.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: walk the landing field, identify obstacles (cables, big trees), and ask the locals which way the standard pattern goes - usually left, but it varies.

Practical Flying_19.explanation: Permanent obstacles like overhead lines and cableways are catalogued on the federal aerial obstacle map (Luftfahrthindernisverzeichnis) and many of them also appear directly on the glider/gliding map. DABS and NOTAM publish only short-term, time-limited information; the VLK regulations are about aircraft categories, not obstacles.

Practical Flying_20.explanation: Sam states the guiding principle bluntly: a launch site is only suitable when an abort remains possible at any moment in the run. A cliff with a short run that drops away into space fails this test even though it looks dramatic and steep. Wind direction, slope angle and windsock are secondary - if you cannot stop, you should not be there.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

diagrams.title: Takeoff: decision line and point of no return

video_clips.snippet: Sam: from every launch site it must be possible at any time to safely abort the take-off - if you can't stop, the site isn't right.

Practical Flying_21.explanation: Airspeed is what makes the wing fly; the ground roll just has to add enough speed-over-ground to reach that airspeed. With 15 km/h of head wind already on the wing, you only need to run up another ~10 km/h of airspeed to lift off, so the take-off run is short and your groundspeed at lift-off is modest. Strong head wind = short run, low ground speed.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

Practical Flying_23.explanation: Zero-wind launches are forward launches in which all the airspeed has to be built by running. The area in front of the glider (the actual run-out) therefore has to be flat, smooth and obstacle-free so you can accelerate without tripping. What lies behind the wing matters very little once it is overhead.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: nil/light wind needs a forward launch on a clean, obstacle-free run-out so we can build the airspeed by running.

Practical Flying_24.explanation: At 20 km/h head wind you do a reverse launch. Once the wing comes up it can lift you a step or two backwards before you stabilise it, so the critical zone is now behind you, not in front. That ground has to be free of stones and holes - tripping while being dragged backward is the classic injury here.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with 20 km/h head wind we reverse launch; we may run up-slope a step or two toward the wing, so behind us must be obstacle-free.

Practical Flying_25.explanation: Inside a forest corridor the trees shelter the launch and you'll often feel essentially no wind at ground level - while at canopy height the actual ridge wind is blowing. The moment the wing climbs above the tree tops it abruptly meets that wind, often with a hard pull or sudden swing. Plan the launch and the first seconds of climb-out for that transition.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

Practical Flying_26.explanation: On the lee side, air spills over the ridge in rolling eddies rather than as steady flow. The result on the launch is exactly what gives it away: wind that swings rapidly in direction and strength, sometimes blowing up, sometimes down, sometimes from the side. Steady, predictable wind is the windward signature; abrupt shifts mean you're behind the obstacle.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

Practical Flying_27.explanation: At 25 km/h head wind a forward launch is dangerous - the wing comes up far above you and you have very little chance of controlling it. The textbook solution is a reverse launch on a shallow slope: shallow so the wing does not overshoot and pluck you off before you are ready, reverse so you can watch and control the canopy through the gust. Steep slope plus that wind = immediate uncontrolled lift-off.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: 20+ km/h means reverse launch facing the wing, taking care not to be lifted off prematurely.

Practical Flying_28.explanation: Sam goes through the check in the order: body and equipment first (reserve pins, all buckles closed and secured), then risers and lines, then the wing layout, then wind direction and strength, and finally the launch path and airspace - the last item is checked again right before the take-off run.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: 5-point check from inside out - reserve handle and all buckles, risers not twisted with lines free, wing laid out, wind direction and strength, and launch path/airspace clear.

Practical Flying_30.explanation: Of all five points, the airspace check is the only one that can change in the few seconds between setting up and running. A pilot flying past, an animal walking into the corridor or a paraglider you didn't see - all of these can appear after the check. That is why the rule is to look up one more time just before lifting off.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: after the reverse turn, points 1-4 don't really change, but point 5 - launch path and airspace clear - is repeated carefully immediately before take-off.

Practical Flying_34.explanation: The launch decision is yours alone. 'I fly here often' or 'other people are launching' are answers that have killed pilots; the safety of an officially permitted launch site does not transfer to your skill, gear or condition. Only the pilot can judge whether the conditions match their abilities, and the rule is to launch only when convinced you can do so without problem.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

diagrams.title: Takeoff: decision line and point of no return

video_clips.snippet: Sam: take your time, work calmly, never hectically - especially if you are uncertain and still internalising the launch process.

Practical Flying_36.explanation: The launch has three phases: inflation, control/correction, and acceleration. An abort decision belongs in the control phase - the moment you decide 'no, something is wrong' before you commit to the run. Once you are in the acceleration phase you are committed; trying to abort during the sprint is what produces injuries.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

diagrams.title: Takeoff: decision line and point of no return

video_clips.snippet: Sam: do phase 1 (inflation) and phase 2 (check and correct) cleanly, then decide; aborting in phase 3 means phase 2 wasn't done properly and costs a penalty point in the exam.

Practical Flying_37.explanation: Wind from 120 deg on a south-facing slope means it comes from your left at about 60 deg off the fall line. The standard solution: inflate against the wind so the canopy comes up clean and symmetric, then gradually pivot the wing back toward the fall line while you accelerate down the slope. Trying to launch and accelerate directly into the cross component is a recipe for a slide and a side collapse.

workbook_chapter.title: Pre-flight check and the three-phase launch

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with a bit of crosswind, pull the wing up against the wind first and then run a small distance in the fall direction, with no obstacles to trip on.

Practical Flying_40.explanation: In the first seconds after lift-off you are flying low, slow and inside ground-level turbulence. If a gust collapses one side of the wing while your hands are up, you have no time to brake-recover before you reach the ground. Keep the brakes engaged ready to react - sliding into the harness can wait until you have altitude and clean air below you.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: don't release the brakes right after launch - only sit into the harness once you are flying far, far straight with plenty of air below.

Practical Flying_41.explanation: A knot on the left brings the left wingtip down a bit, increases the angle of attack on that side and produces extra drag - so the wing veers left. To fly straight you have to brake the right (open) side hard. Strong side wind would produce a body drift, not a constant pull to one side.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if you fly out crooked there are several causes - launch line not in the fall line, an unseen knot, or strong crosswind; stabilise first with weight shift and opposite brake, then diagnose.

Practical Flying_42.explanation: A knot in the rear (D-line/brake-line) area shortens that side and effectively pre-brakes it. The angle of attack stays high, so the parachutal-stall tendency is increased; if you also turn into the knotted side the inside brake will reach stall point much sooner than normal. Plan a wide, gentle turn pattern and land using the rear riser of the knotted side rather than full brake.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: tools for opening a knot - brake impulse, pull the stabilo line, even a controlled 50% collapse to unload that side; if it doesn't open and you can fly straight, you decide whether to land like that.

Practical Flying_43.explanation: A knot in the front A-lines pulls the leading edge of that side downward, reducing the angle of attack and the internal pressure on that half of the wing. That side is then much more vulnerable to turbulence and will collapse more easily than the open side - the opposite problem to a rear-line knot.

Practical Flying_44.explanation: A jammed brake is treated as a steering failure. The first priority is to fly away from the slope on the rear riser of the affected side, which still steers and decelerates the wing without the broken brake. Pumping the brakes or throwing the reserve over an unstable, uncollapsed wing makes things worse; turning straight into the hill is the worst option of all.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the absolute priority after launch is right stabilisation - weight to the side, dosed opposite brake, fly straight far out and build distance before tackling the problem.

Practical Flying_45.explanation: In calm air the wing's best glide is at trim speed - arms up, brakes just touching. Any extra brake increases the angle of attack and the sink rate without buying speed; accelerator increases speed but the polar drops faster than glide ratio improves. So calm air, maximum distance: zero brake at trim speed.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: for best glide on a school wing, the brakes are at 5-15 cm of contact - that's trim speed; deeper brake gives minimum sink, not best glide.

Practical Flying_46.explanation: Maximum distance over ground = glide ratio x altitude lost. You start 1200 m above the valley floor (1600 - 400); with the wing's best glide of 9 you can cover 9 x 1.2 km = 10.8 km in calm air. The other distances come from the wrong altitude difference or the wrong arithmetic.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_48.explanation: Your body is the biggest drag-producing object hanging under the wing, so the polar genuinely changes with posture. A compact, reclined position with arms tight to the risers cuts pressure drag and adds 1-2 km/h. Extended legs or shoulders pushed forward actually move the centre of gravity backward and increase drag - which is why a clean aerodynamic position is part of the syllabus.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: sit compactly and you glide well with little drag - or sit up and stand out of the harness to add drag and bleed altitude efficiently on final.

Practical Flying_49.explanation: Against a 25 km/h head wind, trim speed gives you almost no progress over ground - the wing crawls. You need to fly the wing fast: trimmers open if you have them, accelerator on. Yes, the sink rate increases, but ground covered per metre lost is greater because you're not standing in the air. This is the classic 'speed up in headwind/sink' rule.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with a 30 km/h headwind on a 30 km/h wing you'd stand still - thank goodness you can accelerate to get another 10 km/h and actually still move forward.

Practical Flying_52.explanation: Wind from the west at 25 km/h would drift the wing east of the target. To still reach a landing exactly south, you have to angle the heading into the wind component (south-westerly) so that drift plus heading produce a true south track. And because you also have a head-wind component, you must fly fast (accelerator on) to keep the over-ground progress as high as possible.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in crosswind, choose a wind-correction angle - point the longitudinal axis a bit into the wind so the ground track is a straight line to the landing.

Practical Flying_53.explanation: With no wind, aiming the heading directly at the target produces a straight line over ground to the target - the simplest case shown in track 4. The other tracks are crosswind paths or dogleg arcs; they would only happen if there were wind or you constantly readjusted the heading.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_54.explanation: Zero wind, heading due south, target exactly south - this just gives a straight south track (path 3). The west wind in the question stem is 0 km/h, so the geometry collapses to the no-wind case. The visual gotcha is that students sometimes read 'west wind' as 'crosswind' even when its speed is zero.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_55.explanation: Heading south-west with a 20 km/h west wind: the wing slides east because of the crosswind from the west, but the south-west heading already builds in some westerly correction. The vector sum produces the straight-south track to the target shown in path 1 - exactly the standard crab-angle solution.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_56.explanation: The crab angle is purely a geometry term: it is the angle between where the nose of the wing is pointing (longitudinal axis) and where the wing is actually travelling over the ground (the trajectory). Wind direction or aircraft transverse axis don't enter the definition - they only set the size of the angle you need.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the wind-correction angle is nothing other than the angle between the longitudinal axis of the wing and the flight path we choose.

Practical Flying_57.explanation: The harder the side wind blows, the more the wind vector pushes the wing sideways relative to its own airspeed, so the heading has to be cranked further into the wind to compensate. Direction matters too (no wind = no crab) but the question is about magnitude: stronger crosswind always means a bigger correction angle.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_59.explanation: The wind component is fixed; only your airspeed is in your control. Going faster makes the side-wind vector shrink relative to your own velocity, so the heading has to bend into wind less to keep the ground track straight - the crab angle gets smaller. Accelerator-on in strong crosswind also keeps the ground track honest.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_60.explanation: The crab-angle vector triangle is built from three quantities: how fast the wing is going (airspeed), how fast the wind is blowing (strength) and from where (direction). Flight distance and sink rate don't enter the geometry at all - they only tell you how long you stay aloft, not which heading to fly.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_61.explanation: A cumulus is the visible top of a thermal column. With head wind the column tilts away from the pilot (downwind), so by the time you reach the cumulus the rising air is already behind it. The lift you can still tap is therefore on the windward side - between your position and the cloud, not under or downwind of it.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with wind, fly into the upwind side of the cloud so you can climb in the column together with the drift and optimally catch the thermal.

Practical Flying_62.explanation: If you drift 3 km in 10 minutes while circling, the average wind at your altitude is 3 km / (10/60 h) = 18 km/h. You don't need any extra information about ground-level conditions to compute it - the thermal effectively makes you a wind drogue. Stronger ground wind is possible but isn't derivable from the question.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_63.explanation: A vario showing sink after 90 deg of turn typically means you've turned right out of the column. Reversing direction makes you do another 180 deg back to where you came from; flying straight a short way often puts you back into rising air. Continuing the turn in the same direction or instantly switching usually wastes height without finding the core again.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: core the thermal nicely on one hand but really work hard with weight shift; only leave it when you're sure you'll reach the next planned point.

Practical Flying_64.explanation: Without ambient wind there is nothing to push the rising air sideways - the thermal goes straight up like a chimney. Any inclination of the column always traces to a horizontal wind component; remove the wind and the column is vertical.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_65.explanation: Mass conservation: the air rising in the thermal has to be replenished from the sides, and it then sinks back down outside the column. So the thermal is surrounded by a halo of compensating sink. Leave the core and you don't merely 'stop climbing' - you start descending faster than normal sink. That's why centring matters so much.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_66.explanation: Strong sink right after losing the lift means you've drifted out of the column and into the compensating sink around it. A tight 120-180 deg turn brings you back into the core faster than a flat half-circle and much faster than searching for new lift. Flat turns lose altitude in sink; flying straight just continues the bleed.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_67.explanation: A wind from 270 deg is a westerly. The thermal column drifts with the wind, so it tilts in the direction the wind is blowing - toward the east. That's why on a westerly day you fly to the west of the visible cumulus to find the core at higher altitudes.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_68.explanation: The compensating sink around a tilted thermal isn't symmetric: it is strongest on the leeward (downwind) side, because that is where the air spills off the tilted column. Cross the thermal on the lee side and the sink is much harder than on the windward side - good incentive to stay upwind of the core.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_69.explanation: Since a tilted column has stronger sink on the lee side, staying upwind of the core when circling keeps you in the column for longer as both you and it drift downwind together. The choice of turn direction matters much less than knowing where the strong sink lives.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_70.explanation: On XC, leaving a thermal is a planned decision, not a habit. You commit to the next objective - cumulus, ridge, possible source - and you stay in the climb until your altitude on the polar reaches that point with margin. Leaving at cloudbase or when the climb dies are habits that cost you the day.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: only leave the thermal when you're sure you'll reach the next chosen point - core it out, take patience, and arrive with the altitude you need.

Practical Flying_71.explanation: Thermal turbulence next to a ridge means thermals are detaching and rolling against the slope. The defensive answer is twofold: widen your distance to the terrain so a collapse never puts you in rock contact, and trim your airspeed to suit the conditions (a touch of speed bar usually makes the wing fly through gusts more cleanly). Releasing trimmers or quitting soaring entirely is not the standard procedure.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

video_clips.snippet: Sam: terrain clearance, terrain clearance and again terrain clearance - and always turn the curve away from the slope, never toward it.

Practical Flying_73.explanation: Every wing produces wingtip vortices and a churned-up trail behind it. For a paraglider the wake is short-lived but very real - fly close behind another wing, especially near the ground, and you can feel the rolling sink and pitch upsets. The polite spacing is the same as for any aircraft: a generous offset so you stay out of the lead pilot's wake.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_75.explanation: Pulling both brakes too hard takes airspeed away symmetrically, so the airflow detaches at the same moment on both sides of the wing. With no airflow the wing either hangs vertically with no forward speed (parachutal/inflated stall) or tips backwards and deflates (full stall). Spiral and front collapse happen from asymmetric input, not symmetric over-braking.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: brake too deep and you reach the stall point - the wing goes from forward flight to deep stall and possibly into full stall behind you.

Practical Flying_76.explanation: In a parachutal stall the wing keeps its span but stops moving forward. The classic indicators all show the loss of airflow: no wind in your face, slack brake pressure that gives no resistance, and the underside fabric ballooning concave because the relative wind now hits it from below. High sink without brake pressure is the giveaway.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: at the stall point brake pressure suddenly drops like a pistol shot; visually you see the lower surface bulging upward because the air now pushes on it from below.

Practical Flying_77.explanation: Out of parachutal stall at altitude you have to give the wing speed again. Pushing forward on the A-risers or stepping on the accelerator tilts the canopy nose down, restores forward flight and resolves the stall. Pumping brakes either side risks driving the wing into full stall or spin; waiting for a self-recovery is unsafe because some parachutal stalls are stable.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: stuck in deep stall - move forward to the A-risers, pull, release; that's the exit to forward flight.

Practical Flying_78.explanation: Ten metres above the ground you have no altitude to recover a forward surge. The least bad option is to keep the wing in parachutal stall (it descends roughly vertically at controlled sink), sit upright, get the legs ready and execute a parachute landing fall. Pumping brakes near the ground would cause a forward surge into the ground; throwing the reserve from 10 m is too low to inflate.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in parachutal stall we have no forward speed - the cure is fast: hands on the A-risers and push, or hit the accelerator, to get the wing back into normal flight.

Practical Flying_79.explanation: All three are well-documented routes into a stable parachutal stall: stretched (out-of-trim) lines from age, brakes set too short at the line lock, and rain-soaked fabric (heavier wing, distorted profile). That's why annual line trim checks, correct brake-line length and dropping into landing instead of climbing into showers all matter for safety.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Sam: parachutal stall is just flying too slowly - reasons can include flying with a very wet wing or having the brake too far down.

Practical Flying_80.explanation: In a turn, the inside half of the wing flies slower than the outside half. Pull the inside brake too hard and the inside half drops below stall speed first - airflow detaches there, the wing whips over to that side and the asymmetric stall develops into a spin. Symmetric over-braking from straight flight goes to parachutal stall; this is different.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Sam: a spin happens when you pull one side down too much - typically over-braking the inside to land - and the wing turns negatively while you follow underneath, sometimes into a twist.

Practical Flying_81.explanation: An abrupt turn input from straight flight is essentially the same as suddenly over-braking one side: the inside half of the wing loses airspeed too quickly, the airflow separates and the wing pitches over and starts to spin. Smooth turn initiation with weight shift first, then dosed brake, keeps you well inside the stable envelope.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in a turn put weight on first, then take the brake along to support - work slowly with the brake so you build the bank progressively, not abruptly.

Practical Flying_82.explanation: When the inside of the wing stalls, the airflow on that half collapses and the brake line goes slack: you can pull it almost to your hip without ever feeling pressure. That sudden 'nothing there' on the inside brake is the unmistakable signature. Spiral builds G-forces; pendulum is about pitch; a side collapse releases pressure differently.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: brake pressure rises as you pull deeper, then drops like a pistol shot when the stall point is reached - immediately hands up.

Practical Flying_83.explanation: Once one side has stalled, the recipe is the same as for any stall: release brake pressure so the wing can take a clean profile and accelerate. Both hands up lets the airflow reattach and the wing return to normal flight. Pumping the inside brake just deepens the stall; loading the outside brake or grabbing the risers can drive you into a spin or twist.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: if we've stalled the wing - keep the hands up, let the wing fly, and only react if a collapse develops.

Practical Flying_84.explanation: Asymmetric stall risk is minimised by keeping the wing well above its stall speed - 'reserve speed' - and by flying with fingertip feel rather than hauling the brakes. You never lose much by being a bit faster; you can lose everything by being a bit too slow. Anemometers measure groundspeed and brake/turn rules don't replace airspeed margin.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

Practical Flying_85.explanation: Pulling only the A-risers does the opposite of accelerating: it tips the leading edge downward, drops the angle of attack on every cell at once, and the wing folds inward as the wingtips swing forward past the centre. That's a textbook front (frontal) collapse. The accelerator does the same job safely because it pulls the A and B groups together in a coordinated way.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

Practical Flying_86.explanation: When more than half the wing folds away, the asymmetric drag and lift loss start a rotation toward the collapsed side. If the pilot does nothing, that rotation accelerates - the wing tips dive and the rotation tightens into a constantly increasing spiral. Active counter-braking and weight shift are needed; passive 'wait and see' usually ends badly.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: an asymmetric collapse brakes that side - the wing wants to rotate; we want to fly straight, so we counter with weight and dosed opposite brake.

Practical Flying_87.explanation: The procedure on a side collapse is sequential, not simultaneous: first stabilise the direction with weight shift toward the open side and a gentle dose of brake on the open side so the wing flies straight, then - and only then - pump the collapsed side if it hasn't reopened by itself. Full brake on either side, or symmetric full brake, all risk stalling on top of the collapse.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: work carefully with the open-side brake, seek straight heading and keep flying for a few seconds; if the collapse doesn't come out by itself, pump it - but always with two-three second spacing.

Practical Flying_88.explanation: A bit of pendulum motion in turbulence is normal. The right response is to take the brakes down to about 25-30 % and just feel the wing - active 'fight back' braking risks introducing the wing into oscillation cycles. Sit relaxed, let your body absorb the motion, and the wing self-stabilises. Aggressive opposite brake and high speed both make things worse.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: holding the brake with a short outward wrap gives you very good feel and a short steering travel, much less strenuous in turbulence.

Practical Flying_91.explanation: A wingover is a deliberate, rhythmic alternation of turns that builds energy each cycle until the wing momentarily exceeds 90 deg of bank. It is a swinging manoeuvre around the longitudinal axis - very different from a spiral (constant tight turn) or a twist (risers cross). The exam requires demonstrating wingovers to stabilo height.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: rolling is the motion around the longitudinal axis - body first, then dosed brake, weight onto the new side at the highest point and the brake comes shortly after the lowest point.

Practical Flying_92.explanation: A spiral dive is a sustained tight turn in which the wing screws downward and the load factor climbs as centrifugal force grows. 4 G is realistic on a school wing - twice your normal weight pressing on you. That's why the safety syllabus reserves it for training under supervision: it descends fast but punishes the body.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in a spiral dive G-forces increase - 100 kg can become 400 kg at 4 G; this is why you only learn it under guidance, at SIV or on a G-Force trainer.

Practical Flying_93.explanation: Big ears is the rapid-descent of choice when you also need horizontal control. The wing still flies forward (you only fold the outer A-line in), the body doesn't take G-forces and weight-shift steering still works - so you can leave a lift band, head into the wind, or pick a landing field. The other rapid-descent options (B-stall, spiral) either descend on the spot or load the body hard.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: big-ears gives very high sink rates already in straight flight, with weight shift even double-digit m/s, very easily and without physical strain.

Practical Flying_95.explanation: All three side notes apply to big-ears: only the intended outer A-lines must be pulled (others on the A-row would fold the centre and front-collapse the wing); a clumsy grab can pick up the middle A-line and produce that frontal collapse; and inexperienced pilots can take the ears in one at a time to avoid pulling both centre lines together.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: don't pull too low at the line lock - you would drag the innermost A-line down and produce a frontal; grab high above the lock and kink them first.

Practical Flying_96.explanation: Folding the wingtips in cuts surface area but keeps your weight the same, so wing loading goes up and forward speed drops. The pilot has to fly at a higher angle of attack than usual, which puts the wing much closer to its stall point than in normal flight. That's why entering and especially exiting big ears can drop you into a parachutal stall if you're not on the bar.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: with ears in, wing loading rises, the angle of attack rises and the stall point shifts to higher speed - that's why we always combine ears with the accelerator.

Practical Flying_97.explanation: The accelerator is what tilts the canopy forward enough to keep angle of attack out of the stall band when ears are in. The combination is therefore safe: ears for area reduction (high sink) plus accelerator to keep airflow attached. The notion that they should not be combined is exactly the wrong way around.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: by accelerating, the canopy tilts forward and down, the angle of attack decreases and the stall window widens again - that's why ears always go with the accelerator.

Practical Flying_98.explanation: Close to the ground you don't want a forward surge or a brake-induced stall. Pumping the brakes to pop the ears back open could either over-brake the now slow-flying wing into parachutal stall or trigger a pitch surge into the ground. So at low altitude let them open passively (release the A-lines, weight shift) and keep flying - never actively pump on short final.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: stay fully on the bar, release the A-lines symmetrically, only if needed pump them - but with full bar - and only come off the accelerator once the ears are fully out.

Practical Flying_99.explanation: Spiral dive achieves a very high sink rate, but at the cost of sustained G-forces of 2-4 G on the body. After a minute or two many pilots blackout or vomit; for older pilots the load is genuinely dangerous. That's why it isn't a 'go-to' descent manoeuvre - big ears with bar is gentler and works for everyone.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: steep spiral isn't suitable for losing altitude over a longer period, especially on today's wings - the physical load is just too much.

Practical Flying_100.explanation: In a stable spiral you keep both speed and G-load under control by feeding outside brake and shifting weight outwards. Releasing the inside brake alone might let the wing reopen too suddenly; the outside brake is what tames the rotation while the inside hand stays planted. The myth that spiral exits cause parachutal stall is wrong - the issue is over-shoot and G-spike.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: never just release the inner brake - keep it and pull the outer brake until you come back out of the bank angle.

Practical Flying_101.explanation: All three caveats are textbook: an established spiral has to be actively controlled with outside brake and outside weight; depending on harness/glider/body geometry it can become 'stable' and the wing won't exit on its own; lifting the outer leg over the inner tightens the spin and increases the dive tendency. That's why spiral training belongs in SIV, not in everyday flying.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

Practical Flying_104.explanation: Landing up the slope brings you against a rising surface that effectively cuts your touchdown speed to zero very fast - the geometry makes pinpoint placement easy. The drawback is that the same wedge of ground is decelerating you violently, so the impact is harsh on legs and spine. Down-slope landings keep the contact gentler but the run-out is uncontrolled.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: a slope landing has to be parallel to the slope; flying straight into the slope wrecks the flare calculation.

Practical Flying_105.explanation: The landing volte is not just a beginner crutch - it's a shared protocol. Everyone in the pattern can predict where everyone else is going, so collisions on short final are avoided, and the geometry happens to be the best for hitting a spot accurately. That's why licensed pilots still fly it, including on outlandings.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: when landing everything happens faster because the ground is close - we all stick to the landing pattern so each pilot can see what the one ahead is doing.

Practical Flying_106.explanation: In the standard volte the first phase, #1, is the altitude-loss zone (position circles): you circle in the same direction as your downwind leg until you've bled off enough height to enter the pattern at a healthy altitude. Only then do you fly off into the downwind leg.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the pattern starts with position (holding) circles in the same direction as the pattern, until you're at a healthy altitude.

Practical Flying_107.explanation: #2 in the diagram is the downwind leg - flown along the side of the landing field with the wind from behind, at a constant safe altitude. From here you set up the base leg perpendicular and finally turn final into wind.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: don't use visual cues like tree height - it changes with the air mass; downwind, base, final, learn it by feel.

Practical Flying_108.explanation: #3 is the base leg - the crosswind segment that connects the downwind leg to the final approach. It is the only segment of the volte you can stretch (you're too high) or shorten (you're too low) to fine-tune your descent path.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: too high on downwind - extend or repeat the base with S-turns; too low - shorten or skip it.

Practical Flying_109.explanation: #4 is the final leg - the straight segment from the end of base into wind to the landing spot. It must be flown as straight as you can manage, with only small wind-correction adjustments so you don't drift sideways into another pilot's flare zone.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: final should be straight - small corrections only if the wind comes from an angle - flying straight toward the landing point.

Practical Flying_110.explanation: Always circle in the same sense as the pattern itself so other pilots can predict your motion - if you'll fly a left-hand pattern, do left position circles. Random left-or-right circles, S-turns or contradictory directions all break the predictability that the volte is supposed to provide.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: right-hand pattern means right-hand circles, left-hand pattern means left-hand circles - then we go to a healthy altitude to enter the pattern.

Practical Flying_111.explanation: For a left-hand approach the altitude-loss circles are placed in sector 2 of the diagram, so that you naturally end up on the upwind side and can fly the left downwind leg parallel to the landing field. Any other sector forces you to cross your own pattern to enter the downwind leg.

Practical Flying_113.explanation: Too low on the turn from downwind to base means you have no spare height to spend - so the right correction is to cut the corner: turn directly toward the final aim point rather than continuing to the planned base position. Diagram 2 shows that shortened path; anything that extends the route loses you the rest of your altitude.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: too short - shorten the base or skip it entirely; the S-turn option only works when you have altitude to spare.

Practical Flying_114.explanation: Too high on the base leg means too much energy remaining. Lengthen the base leg by flying farther past the planned turn-onto-final, possibly add S-turns, and only then turn final - diagram 4 shows that extended base. Tightening the path makes the height excess even worse.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: too high on downwind - turn base early and extend it far forward; the S-turns let you bleed extra altitude on a clean line.

Practical Flying_115.explanation: With 10-15 km/h of wind, the standard volte (diagram 1) lines up exactly: downwind along the side of the field, short crosswind base, final straight into wind. The 'standard pattern is the right pattern' is exactly what these diagrams are designed to reinforce.

Practical Flying_116.explanation: In very light or zero wind the wing covers the same distance per metre of altitude no matter which way it points. The pattern has to be opened out (wider downwind, longer base) so you don't arrive too high - diagram 3 shows that expanded pattern. A tight standard volte at 0-5 km/h would put you well above the field on final.

Practical Flying_117.explanation: First pilot down picks the direction. Convention here defaults to the standard left-hand pattern, which lets the followers copy it without negotiation. Forcing a 90-degree-to-valley-axis landing or simply 'free choice' both lead to chaos when the next pilots are only 50 m behind.

Practical Flying_118.explanation: You've already committed to the pattern, and the two pilots behind you have committed to copy it. With a 2 m/s tailwind on what was going to be your final, the glide is flatter and your touchdown will overshoot - so the right adjustment is a shallower final, not a steeper one, and an earlier flare. Abandoning the pattern mid-base or landing across the wind invites collisions with the others.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if the wind shifts and final becomes downwind, follow through with the pattern you chose - tailwind landing means more speed on final, brake decisively, but don't bail mid-pattern.

Practical Flying_119.explanation: With tailwind, ground speed equals airspeed plus wind speed. The wing crosses the ground faster, so the glide angle relative to the ground is shallower (it 'travels further per metre lost') - and you touch down with higher speed than airspeed alone, making the run-out harder. Both effects work against soft landings.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with a tailwind you have more speed on final - you have to brake through more decisively, higher up, to build the moment when the wing stands up and you're on the ground.

Practical Flying_120.explanation: Head wind subtracts from the ground speed, so the wing comes down at a steeper angle and touches down with lower over-ground speed. This is the easy case: short flare, gentle run-out, exactly what the volte is optimised for. Stronger headwind = steeper, slower, kinder landing.

Practical Flying_123.explanation: If you arrive at the base leg with too much height, the cleanest answer is to fly the base segment longer - and if necessary repeat it with S-turns - so the altitude bleeds off on a predictable, straight track. Returning to the holding pattern wastes time, circling on base risks others, and pulling brakes reduces glide but increases stall risk.

Practical Flying_124.explanation: Too low on the base leg means you have to head directly to the field. Shortening or abandoning the base entirely lets you turn final immediately and arrive with whatever altitude is left. Trying to improve glide with brakes actually steepens the descent; circling at low altitude crashes you out.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if we're too short, we can also shorten the whole thing - the S-turns and base length are how we manage altitude on the way to final.

Practical Flying_125.explanation: A small height excess on short final is corrected by adding drag from your body: brake the wing slightly to lower the glide ratio, and stand up out of the harness so the seat board, legs and torso work as a parachute. Acceleration would do the opposite (less drag, more glide), and S-turning or U-turning at low altitude is dangerous.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: too high - make yourself big, sit up out of the harness with legs ready, a lot of drag, poor glide - the altitude bleeds off efficiently.

Practical Flying_126.explanation: Already too low on base, you need maximum glide: keep the brakes off and the body compact in the harness so the wing flies the polar's flattest path. Only just before touchdown do you slowly and continuously start to brake for the flare. Heavy brake or 50% drag would steepen the descent and you'd land short.

Practical Flying_127.explanation: 80% brake puts the wing right at the edge of the stall envelope. The polar is at minimum sink but the wing is flying slowly with high angle of attack. Any gust or tiny brake increase will drop airflow off the wing and you fall into parachutal or full stall. That's why 80% brake is fine for a moment in the flare but not a sustained state.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: brake pressure rises as you pull deeper, but the closer to the stall point the more dangerous - a small extra input releases the pressure and the wing has stalled.

Practical Flying_128.explanation: The landing area belongs to whoever comes next. Folding, removing gear or filling in the logbook all happen off the landing strip. Step off the field with your wing as soon as you can - the pilot behind you is already on final.

Practical Flying_129.explanation: At 25 km/h, brake pulls only make the wing produce more lift and drag you. The classic procedure is the opposite: spin 180 deg to face the wing, pull on the B or C-line group (or rear risers) to collapse the lifting surface from the back, and run forward toward the wing. Pulling A-risers can cause an asymmetric collapse that drags you sideways.

Practical Flying_130.explanation: On a steep slope, landing straight uphill or downhill leaves no margin in the flare - the slope geometry wrecks your reference. Approaching parallel to the contour lets you touch down with the slope just to your side, flare normally, and step off cleanly. Landing 'against the wind' is the rule on flat ground but is overridden by the slope on terrain.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: a slope landing must be parallel to the slope - if you fly straight at the slope, the inclination wrecks your flare calculation.

Practical Flying_131.explanation: In valley wind, mechanical turbulence is produced by anything upstream of the field - trees, buildings, ridges. The most important criterion is therefore that the windward side is flat and obstacle-free, so the air arrives at the landing point smoothly. Sheltered or wind-shadow fields actually create rotor zones over the landing area.

Practical Flying_132.explanation: Buildings shed rotor turbulence on their downwind side that extends a long way and reaches all the way down to ground level - that's why you can be pummelled for the entire final, not just at roof height. Don't expect a calm ground-level pocket and don't expect ground-effect float to save you; plan an aggressive flare with margin.

Practical Flying_133.explanation: A wind gradient on final means the wing meets slower air as you descend - it briefly flies above stall but with reduced internal pressure and can sag back. The correct response is to leave the brakes alone (extra brake stalls it cleanly) and let the wing re-accelerate, only braking gently to flare. Full brake here drops you straight in.

diagrams.title: Full stall vs parachutal stall vs spin

Practical Flying_134.explanation: Water landings are life-threatening because the harness and lines tangle around you. The standard mitigation is to open as many harness buckles as possible while still airborne (chest strap, leg straps) so that on contact you can immediately fall out and away from the gear. Tightening straps locks you to the sinking wing; throwing reserves over water is a flotation myth.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: as early as possible, when you see you'll go into the water, open the harness buckles so you can fly away from the gear immediately - but never jump out before you hit the water.

Practical Flying_135.explanation: Falling out of the tree is the single biggest cause of serious injury in tree landings. The wing is usually caught in the branches and supports you safely; impatient pilots who unbuckle to climb down or wait for branches to give way fall the last few metres onto roots and rocks. Stay clipped in and wait for rescue.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: most tree-rescue injuries happen because the pilot tries to free themselves and falls - stay calm, stay where you are, call mountain rescue.

Practical Flying_136.explanation: To minimise damage when retrieving the wing from the crown, the gentlest method is to undo the quick-links connecting the risers to the harness and untie the brake lines. The wing can then be lifted out vertically without dragging it through the branches. Disconnecting harness-to-riser still pulls fabric down; helicopters and rope-yanks cause heavy damage.

Practical Flying_137.explanation: Delta pilots have a poor blind-spot looking up and forward; paraglider pilots have a poor blind-spot looking down and behind. A delta closing from behind and below combines both blind spots - neither pilot can see the other - and the delta's higher speed closes the gap quickly. This is statistically the most dangerous co-altitude scenario in mixed traffic.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

Practical Flying_138.explanation: In a shared thermal you respect the existing rotation direction - both pilots can predict each other, and the spacing stays roughly constant. Suddenly reversing your turn direction sets you and the delta on intersecting paths at the same altitude with closure speeds that leave no time to react. The cardinal rule is: first one in sets the direction, everybody follows.

guide_chapter.title: Range and Optimization of Flight, Lateral Drift, Flight in Lift

Practical Flying_139.explanation: The reserve is a 'second chance' tool with one rule: deploy whenever the situation appears uncontrollable and won't stabilise within 2-3 seconds. Asymmetric collapses on final, cloud suction or even a single full frontal are still situations a trained pilot can manage; uncontrollable is the threshold.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the reserve is a second chance - the principle is: out of control, situation doesn't stabilise within 2-3 seconds, low altitude with a problem, mid-air collision - pull immediately.

Practical Flying_140.explanation: A delta-paraglider head-on collision is an instant non-controllable situation: the wing is shredded or entangled with another aircraft, you have no idea of damage and the closure rate has put you in freefall. Pull the reserve immediately - the other options (cravat, spin, cloud climb) are recoverable with technique and altitude.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents
