video_clips.snippet: Sam: if we have a collision in the air it's also definitely time to pull the reserve - we don't know how badly damaged our aircraft is.

Practical Flying_141.explanation: A twisted-line spiral after a spin is the classic 'uncontrollable and getting worse' situation: you can't reach the brakes through the twist, the wing is in an accelerating dive and there is no recovery technique that solves both at once. Throw the reserve. A single cravat or one-sided stall in a spin is still flyable with the right inputs.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

video_clips.snippet: Sam: when the wing is no longer controllable and it doesn't stabilise within 2-3 seconds - or worse, you don't know where up and down are - that's reserve time.

Practical Flying_143.explanation: Pale skin, blue lips, cold sweat, weak rapid pulse and confusion are the classic signs of shock - circulatory failure that follows trauma. Position the casualty as they prefer (don't force them flat), shelter them from sun/wind/cold/wet, and give nothing to drink (internal injuries are likely and the stomach must stay empty for surgery). Recovery position is only for the unresponsive but breathing.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

diagrams.title: Accident response: ABC and key actions

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with spinal injury the principle is leave them lying, don't let them drink, immediately call Rega - moving the casualty risks paralysis.

Practical Flying_144.explanation: Unresponsive but breathing means the airway is the next thing to fail. Recovery position (on the side, lower arm forward, knee bent up) keeps the tongue out of the airway and lets vomit drain rather than aspirate. Then shelter from sun/wind/cold/wet and monitor breathing continuously - if it stops, start CPR.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

diagrams.title: Accident response: ABC and key actions

Practical Flying_146.explanation: A spinal cord injury characteristically presents with back pain plus loss of sensation in the limbs - the nerves below the damage level no longer report. Unconsciousness, leg pain alone or shock symptoms can occur with many other injuries. The action is the opposite of moving: immobilise and call Rega.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

diagrams.title: Accident response: ABC and key actions

video_clips.snippet: Sam: spinal injuries are common in paragliding because we sit; if the pilot is hurt in the back and breathing, leave them lying and call Rega.

Practical Flying_147.explanation: Before a rescue helicopter arrives, the area has to be made completely safe for it: stop all paragliding traffic nearby (downwash zones can collapse wings), remove every loose item (gliders, jackets, packs) that could be lifted by the rotors and damage them or hit the casualty. Don't strip the casualty of helmet/harness - the rescue team will manage that.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

video_clips.snippet: Sam: when the helicopter comes it generates a lot of wind - gather the wing with a quick fold, pack everything up and weigh it down so nothing can fly away and endanger the helicopter.

Practical Flying_148.explanation: Golden eagles breed in early spring, March through May. Pairs are monogamous, the territory is large (~100 km^2), and the hen lays one or two eggs in the eyrie. Paraglider passage through the territory during this window is exactly when the female can be flushed off the eggs - which kills the brood.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with eagles you have to expect from March to May that they're sitting on the eyrie and defending their territory; chamois have their young in May, ibex in June.

Practical Flying_149.explanation: The 'garland flight' (Girlandenflug) is a sinuous, undulating display flight that golden eagles use to mark territory boundaries - swooping low then rising, again and again, as a visible warning to rivals. If you see an eagle doing this near you, the message is exactly 'this is mine, my chicks are here, leave': turn away.

Practical Flying_151.explanation: Yes - in officially designated protected zones (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries) outlandings and take-offs are banned by federal regulation plus local agreements. The blanket 'no, anywhere is fine' answer ignores the legal framework; 'all national protected areas' overstates it (regulation is zone-specific).

video_clips.snippet: Sam: there are local wildlife agreements, also on the info board - zones you should not fly into; ask the locals or read the board.

Practical Flying_152.explanation: Eagles are most disturbed when paragliders cross the airspace near their nest during the breeding window. The female flushes off the eggs, the embryos cool, and the brood is lost. Marking-boundary flights and prey hunting carry stress but not the same direct cost to next year's population.

Practical Flying_153.explanation: From winter through early spring, chamois and ibex congregate on the steep south-facing slopes where the snow melts first and they can scrape down to grass. North-facing slopes stay frozen and forested gullies offer no food. So fly that fact into your planning: south slopes in winter/spring carry the densest wildlife.

Practical Flying_155.explanation: Alpine huts, holiday chalets and surrounding land are private property even when their owners are absent. The flying-fee covers the launch site, not the entire mountain. Respecting privacy and not bringing flying neighbours into conflict is the only sustainable approach if you want the site to stay open in the long run.

Practical Flying_156.explanation: End-of-winter snow still covers the grass, while fat reserves accumulated in autumn are spent and the animals can't yet feed. March-April is when most chamois and ibex die - so they are also most fragile then. A flush at that point can finish an animal that would have made it through with just two more weeks.

Practical Flying_157.explanation: Summer routine for chamois/ibex: graze the high pastures in the cool morning, then retreat to shaded cliffs and wind-exposed knolls above the pastures to rest through the hot midday. That's exactly where soaring paragliders go - the overlap is the source of the conflict.

Practical Flying_158.explanation: The worst disturbance is flying close along mountain slopes and cliffs above the tree line, in remote areas where humans rarely appear - exactly the terrain animals retreat to expecting safety. The unexpected airborne predator silhouette triggers flight, which costs energy and exposes them to snow patches/avalanches.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if you see animals, fly away from them - don't tell yourself the thermal is too good, the animals already have enough stress from the environment.

Practical Flying_159.explanation: The most effective wildlife protection a single pilot can practice is to follow the negotiated agreements between clubs and gamekeepers - they already encode local seasonal sensitivities, no-fly zones and times. Reacting to fleeing animals is reactive, shouting is counterproductive and flying low is just more disturbance.

Practical Flying_160.explanation: Helicopter rescues need the landing area completely clear. Pack up everything you've brought and walk out to a safe distance where wind/rotor wash can't catch your gear and send it through the rotor disc - that endangers the crew and the patient. Leaving gear behind risks it being lifted, which is worse than not moving at all.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the helicopter generates a lot of wind - gather the wing, pack everything, weigh things down so nothing can fly away.

Practical Flying_162.explanation: If anyone has seen your reserve descent and reported a likely crash, the Rega will already be coordinating a rescue you don't need. Calling Rega on 1414 with your position and 'unhurt' avoids a wasted, expensive, dangerous helicopter mission. Just packing up and going home leaves searchers continuing for hours.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

diagrams.title: Accident response: ABC and key actions

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if you're in a civilised area inform mountain rescue or Rega - they probably already know about it and you save them the search.

Practical Flying_164.explanation: A paraglider silhouette against the valley is small and stationary; helicopter pilots scanning a wide field of view can easily miss you. The right reaction is to make yourself visible: small pitch or roll oscillations, or a tighter circle, change your silhouette and motion so the helicopter sees you. Phone calls take too long and big ears don't change visibility.

guide_chapter.title: Flight Patterns, Exit from the Flight Area, Flight Incidents

Practical Flying_165.explanation: A rescue helicopter at the landing field means the field is closed. The right behaviour is to delay your take-off until the rescue completes and the helicopter is clear, and to broadcast the situation to other pilots on launch so nobody else accidentally tries to land into the rescue. Adding more aircraft to the situation - even 'to help' - just gets in the way.

guide_chapter.title: Physiology, Pathology, Trauma and First Aid

Practical Flying_167.explanation: The 6x6 m red cloth with a diagonal yellow cross is the standard ICAO landing-prohibited symbol. Placed on a landing field it tells every approaching aircraft - paragliders, deltas, helicopters and small fixed-wing - that landings are forbidden right now (typically because of an emergency, hazard or test). Find another field.

Practical Flying_168.explanation: Wildlife and forest protection zones are not just polite suggestions - failure to respect them is the most common reason that flying sites are closed by the municipality or the cantonal authority. The whole pilot community then loses a launch. So 'yes, observe them' is the answer that protects your own flying future.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: wildlife protection used to be a side issue but new studies show paragliders do disturb wildlife - respect local agreements or you jeopardise the flying site itself.

Weather_1.explanation: Dry tropospheric air is roughly 78 percent nitrogen and 21 percent oxygen, with the remaining ~1 percent made up of carbon dioxide, water vapour and noble gases such as helium and argon. Memorise the nitrogen/oxygen split exactly: every variant of this question on the SHV exam is built on those two numbers being the right way round.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: for the theory exam, remember 21% oxygen, 78% nitrogen and 1% noble gases in the troposphere.

Weather_3.explanation: The ICAO standard atmosphere defines sea-level conditions as 1013.25 hPa, 15°C, with a temperature gradient of 0.65°C per 100 m (i.e. 6.5°C per 1000 m). These three numbers anchor every altimeter setting, density calculation and exam question — there is no rounding to 1°C/100 m and no 1033 hPa value.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: standard atmosphere = 1013.25 hPa, 15°C at sea level, 0.65°C per 100 m lapse rate, tropopause at 11 km.

Weather_4.explanation: At our latitudes (Europe) the tropopause sits at about 11 km above sea level — slightly thinner at the poles (~8 km) and thicker at the equator (~18 km), and a touch lower in winter when the cold troposphere shrinks. All weather and all paragliding happens beneath that boundary.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at our latitudes the troposphere is about 11–13 km thick; in the standard atmosphere the tropopause is at 11 km.

Weather_5.explanation: Atmospheric pressure is simply the weight of the column of air above pressed down by gravity. That is why pressure is highest at sea level (longest air column) and decreases steadily with altitude — it has nothing to do with the magnetic poles, dust or Earth's rotation.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: air pressure is the column of air above us, just like water pressure when you dive — gravity does the work.

Weather_6.explanation: Pressure does not fall linearly: it halves every 5500 m. So 1013 hPa at sea level becomes about 500 hPa at 5500 m and roughly a quarter of sea-level pressure (~250 hPa) at 11000 m. The 80 hPa/100 m and the constant-above-tropopause options are wrong by a wide margin.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: every 5500 m of altitude, air pressure halves — so 506 hPa at 5500 m, a value the exam loves.

Weather_7.explanation: The ICAO standard sea-level pressure used by meteorologists worldwide is 1013.2 hPa (often written 1013.25). 760 hPa would correspond to a value in mmHg, while 1033 and 998 are arbitrary high/low values found around real weather systems but never used as the "standard".

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the standard air pressure number you must memorise — 1013.25 hPa at sea level.

Weather_8.explanation: Sea-level pressure is never constant — weather systems continuously push it up (anticyclones, 1020–1040 hPa) or down (depressions, often 955–990 hPa). Barometers move both ways from the standard 1013 hPa value, depending entirely on the prevailing weather situation.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: pronounced lows in Europe go down to ~955 hPa, strong highs reach 1035 hPa or more — these swings drive our weather.

Weather_9.explanation: Reduced-to-sea-level pressure at any given spot depends on the current weather pattern — the warming or cooling of the overlying air mass, its humidity and the position of nearby highs and lows. Surface characteristics, longitude and tides do not move barometric pressure.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: pressure differences arise because some regions stay warmer or colder than their surroundings for a long time.

Weather_11.explanation: Boyle's law: at 5500 m the air pressure is half the sea-level value. With pressure halved, a balloon expands to twice its original volume, so 5 dm³ at sea level becomes 10 dm³ at 5500 m. (At 11000 m where pressure is a quarter of sea level, the same balloon would reach 20 dm³.)

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: as the balloon rises and pressure halves at 5500 m, its volume doubles — Boyle's law in action.

Weather_13.explanation: The sun's rays pass through the atmosphere almost unimpeded and primarily heat the ground; the ground then warms the thin layer of air in contact with it by conduction and radiation. So near-surface air temperature is governed by ground temperature, not by direct solar radiation on the air particles themselves.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the sun does not warm the air it passes through — it warms the ground, and the ground warms the thin layer of air sitting on it.

Weather_15.explanation: At the same altitude, warm air is less dense (lighter) than cold air — that is exactly why hot-air balloons fly and why thermal bubbles rise. Density depends on temperature, not on the adiabatic lapse rate, and is certainly not the same for warm and cold parcels.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a hot-air balloon rises because warm air is less dense than the surrounding cold air — the same principle drives every thermal.

Weather_16.explanation: A dry-adiabatic parcel that rises without condensing always cools at 1°C per 100 m. The reason is the pressure drop with altitude: as pressure decreases the parcel expands, and that expansion (without exchanging heat with the surroundings) dilutes its energy. The 0.65°C/100 m value applies only to the ambient (environmental) lapse rate, not to a rising bubble.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: as long as it rises dry-adiabatically, the bubble cools by exactly 1°C per 100 m.

Weather_17.explanation: A rising bubble of air cools because the surrounding pressure falls with altitude, so the bubble expands (work is done against lower outside pressure) and its temperature drops. This is purely the adiabatic decompression process — nothing to do with leaving a heat source, with evaporating water, or with the angle of the sun.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the balloon rises the pressure drops, the gas expands, and so the temperature decreases — like the cold air rushing out of a compressed-air gun.

Weather_18.explanation: A sinking air mass moves into higher pressure, so it is compressed: its volume decreases and the same energy is concentrated into a smaller space, raising its temperature. This is the mirror image of a rising parcel and is the mechanism behind Föhn warming on the lee side of mountains.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when an air mass is compressed, like pumping up a tyre, it warms — the opposite of expansion-cooling.

Weather_20.explanation: When temperature in a layer actually rises with altitude — instead of falling — that layer is called an "inversion". Inversions form classically on clear nights as the ground radiates heat away, and they cap thermal activity by removing the buoyancy advantage of a rising bubble.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in winter Zurich is in cold fog while the peaks are warm — that classic temperature-with-altitude reversal is called an inversion.

Weather_21.explanation: When temperature stays the same across an entire layer (no decrease and no increase with altitude), it is called an "isotherm" or isothermal layer. Like an inversion, an isothermal layer also stops thermals because the rising bubble no longer has any buoyancy advantage over the ambient air.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: 6°C in the valley and 6°C on the mountain — that constant stratification is an isothermal layer.

Weather_23.explanation: An inversion is, by definition, an air layer where temperature increases with increasing altitude. It is a "reversal" of the normal tropospheric pattern (where temperature decreases with height) and acts as a stable cap that blocks thermal development.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: inversion = the inverse stratification, cold at the bottom and warm above; thermals stop dead.

Weather_24.explanation: An isotherm is a layer in which the temperature remains constant with altitude — it neither falls nor rises. Like an inversion it is a stable layer that blocks thermals, even though the temperature is not reversed but just flat.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the temperature stays the same from the valley to the peak, that constant air stratification is an isothermal layer.

Weather_25.explanation: Inversions and isothermal layers both act as a lid on thermal updrafts: the rising bubble loses its buoyancy advantage once it enters air that is not colder than itself. They are stable layers and therefore barriers to thermals — they neither trigger nor accelerate convection.

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers
