Weather_26.explanation: The vertical temperature gradient is simply the difference in temperature per 100 m change in altitude. It is the standard meteorological measure of how quickly temperature changes with height through the troposphere (around 0.65°C/100 m on average, 1°C/100 m for a dry adiabat).

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

workbook_chapter.title: How the air really warms up

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a gradient in meteorology always describes a progression with altitude — temperature gradient is degrees per 100 m of altitude.

Weather_28.explanation: The classic visual sign of an inversion in flight is a sharply-bounded layer of haze, dust or fog beneath clear air above — the inversion has trapped all the humidity and pollutants in the lower atmosphere. Cumulus bases, zero dew-point spread or "only on the ground" are all wrong: elevated inversions exist too.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when you see hazy layers below clear air, that is an inversion trapping moisture below the cap.

Weather_29.explanation: A persistent ground-level fog or stratus layer is the textbook visible signature of an inversion in winter — radiation cooling trapped the cold, moist air below the warmer layer above. Cirrus, lenticulars and cumulonimbus all signal completely different processes (high humidity, mountain wave, convective storm).

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the Zurich fog with sunshine on the ski peaks above is the textbook winter inversion — fog below, clear and warmer above.

Weather_32.explanation: In a radio-sounding table, an isothermal layer shows up as two adjacent altitudes that report the same temperature (no change with height). You spot it just by scanning the rows for an identical value between two levels — the constant-temperature column in the source data.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the exam gives you a sounding, just scan for the layer where the same temperature is listed at two altitudes — that is the isothermal.

Weather_33.explanation: Condensation is the phase change from gaseous water vapour to liquid water — the process that produces clouds, fog and dew. It releases latent heat into the surrounding air, which is why moist-adiabatic cooling is gentler than dry-adiabatic cooling.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: condensation = gaseous water vapour turning into tiny liquid droplets — that is what makes clouds and fog.

Weather_34.explanation: Melting (solid → liquid) requires energy: the intermolecular bonds of ice must be broken by adding heat to free the water molecules. The other three transitions listed — vapour to liquid, liquid to solid, vapour to solid — all release energy, not absorb it.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: from solid → liquid → gaseous requires energy, while the opposite direction releases energy as latent heat.

Weather_37.explanation: Going from liquid water to solid ice (freezing) releases energy — the latent heat of fusion is given off to the surroundings. The two energy-absorbing transitions are melting and evaporation; sublimation (solid → gaseous) also absorbs energy. Only the rightward-to-leftward direction on the phase diagram releases heat.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: vapour-to-liquid and liquid-to-solid both release heat — energy comes back out of the system as the molecules bond more tightly.

Weather_39.explanation: The dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled, at constant pressure and water-vapour content, for saturation (100% relative humidity) to be reached and the first liquid water to condense out. It is not the relative humidity itself, and it is not specifically about ground radiation — it is a thermodynamic property of the air.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the dew point is the temperature at which the air starts to condense its water vapour — the condensation level.

Weather_40.explanation: Saturated (humid) rising air cools at less than 1°C per 100 m because condensation releases latent heat back into the parcel and partly offsets the adiabatic cooling. In Swiss lower-troposphere conditions the moist-adiabatic rate is roughly 0.65°C/100 m — clearly less than 1°C/100 m, and never as high as 1.5 or 2°C/100 m.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: moist-adiabatic cooling is roughly 0.65°C per 100 m, because condensation gives back latent heat.

Weather_41.explanation: The moist-adiabatic lapse rate depends most strongly on how much water vapour actually condenses in the rising parcel. The more vapour condenses, the more latent heat is released, the warmer the parcel stays, and the smaller the temperature gradient. It is not driven by relative humidity, temperature offset from the surroundings or solar intensity.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: how much the moist adiabatic differs from 1°C/100 m depends on how much water vapour condenses in the bubble.

Weather_43.explanation: Fluffy cumulus clouds are the visible signature of good thermal activity: each cumulus marks the top of a rising thermal column where moisture has condensed at the cloud base. Stratus = stable, cirrus = high ice, lenticular = strong wind aloft — none of them indicate thermals.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: cumulus clouds form at the top of thermals — they mark where the rising bubbles are condensing.

Weather_44.explanation: Altocumulus castellanus — towered, castle-shaped mid-level clouds — appearing just a couple of hours after sunrise signal an unstable, humid layer at their altitude. They are the classic morning warning sign for afternoon thunderstorms because that mid-level instability will be amplified once the boundary layer fully heats.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a basic rule — when small clouds early in the morning start to grow upward, the day is unstable.

Weather_45.explanation: Sunshine at altitude combined with low-altitude fog or stratus is the textbook signature of a strong, stable inversion in the lower troposphere — typically a winter anticyclone. The fog is trapped beneath the warm layer above, and the sky is blue from the mountain tops.

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: classic winter inversion — Zurich sits in fog while the peaks are warm and sunny.

Weather_46.explanation: Radiation (ground) fog needs a clear sky (so the ground can radiate heat away freely), no wind (so the cold, moist surface layer is not mixed out) and a small temperature–dew-point spread (so only a little cooling is required to reach saturation). Big spreads, cloud cover or a 10 kt wind would each kill the process.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: clear sky, calm wind and humid air at night cool the ground by infrared radiation — the textbook recipe for radiation fog.

Weather_47.explanation: Clouds form when air is cooled, most commonly by being lifted (orographic, thermal or frontal uplift), until its temperature drops to its dew point and water vapour begins to condense. Heating and evaporation, blocked uplift at an inversion, or being inside a low-pressure system are not the universal cloud-formation mechanism.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: as the parcel rises and cools, at the condensation level the water vapour turns into tiny droplets — that is how a cloud forms.

Weather_48.explanation: Cumulus cloud base = the lower edge of the cloud, sitting at the altitude where the rising thermal first cools enough for its temperature to equal its dew point. Above this level condensation continues, building the cloud upward; below it the air remains too warm/dry to condense.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the rising bubble cools dry-adiabatically until it reaches the condensation level — that level is the cumulus base.

Weather_49.explanation: A continuous cloud layer with a base at about 4000 m is classified as altostratus — "alto-" denotes the mid-level altitude band (3000–6000 m) and "stratus" the sheet-like, layered form. Nimbostratus is lower and rain-producing; altocumulus is patchy; cirrostratus sits much higher.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: altostratus and altocumulus are both the alto level — between roughly 3 and 7 km.

Weather_50.explanation: Small patchy clouds with a base around 4500 m are altocumulus — "alto-" for the mid-level altitude band, "cumulus" for the heaped/puffy form. Cirrocumulus would be much higher (above 6000 m), altostratus is a sheet, cumulonimbus a towering storm cloud.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: altocumulus is mid-level cumulus around 4–5 km; alto = the mid storey of the cloud grid.

Weather_52.explanation: Cirrocumulus sits above 6000 m (often higher than 8 km), the highest base of the cumulus family and far above stratocumulus, cumulus or cumulonimbus — the others have bases under 3000 m or extend down to it. Cirro-clouds are also pure ice crystals at that altitude.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: cirro-clouds live above 8 km — really, really high; alto is the mid level around 4–7 km.

Weather_54.explanation: Nimbostratus is the thick, rain-producing layer cloud — "nimbo-" means rain in cloud-naming. It is the classic precipitation cloud of warm fronts and well-established low-pressure systems. Stratocumulus, cumulus humilis and cirrostratus produce, at most, drizzle, no rain or only halos.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: nimbus stands for rain — nimbostratus is the layered cloud that just keeps raining, classic warm-front weather.

Weather_55.explanation: Cirrocumulus sits above 6000 m where temperatures are well below freezing, so the small flake-like puffs are always made entirely of ice crystals — like dust hanging in the upper troposphere. The other listed types are too low to be ice-only: nimbostratus, cumulus humilis and stratocumulus all sit in water-droplet territory.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: cirro-clouds at high altitudes consist purely of ice crystals — it is freezing up there.

Weather_57.explanation: Cumulonimbus is by far the most dangerous cloud type for paragliders and hang-gliders: vertical drafts of over 30 m/s, hail, lightning, gust fronts and even being sucked in to high altitude. Cirrocumulus, cumulus humilis and stratocumulus pose almost no threat by comparison.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: for us the cumulonimbus is the most dangerous cloud — thunderstorm, lightning, hail, all of it.

Weather_58.explanation: Lenticular (lens-shaped) clouds develop when a moist air mass flows over a mountain ridge at a sufficient speed and gets pushed into a standing wave. They form at the wave crests where the rising air condenses, and dissolve again as the air descends — which is why they look stationary in a strong wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: lenticularis needs a mountain range, strong wind and some humidity — the air condenses at the wave crest and evaporates again as it descends.

Weather_59.explanation: Lenticular clouds appear stationary despite very strong winds because they form at the fixed crest of a mountain wave: air condenses entering the crest, evaporates leaving it, so the cloud itself stays put while the wind blows through it. They occur in any wind situation — not only south Föhn — and contain ice as well as water.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: just because a lenticularis stays in place does not mean there is no wind — it is a fixed wave above strong wind.

Weather_60.explanation: A grey curtain hanging from the cloud base toward the ground is the textbook far-visible sign that a cumulus congestus or cumulonimbus is precipitating — the vertical streaks of rain or hail are unmistakable from kilometres away. Vertical extent, dark colour or rising fog are not specific precipitation indicators.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when you see the grey precipitation curtain coming out below, the bucket is being poured out — land immediately.

Weather_61.explanation: Once precipitation is visible under a cumulus congestus or cumulonimbus, evaporative cooling of the falling rain produces very cold, dense air that crashes to the surface as a powerful, gusty downdraft — the gust front. That is exactly the storm phase where outflows spread far ahead of the cloud and cause many paraglider accidents.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the bucket of cold air is dumped out under the storm, the outflow sends very strong gusts kilometres ahead of the cloud — that is the underestimated danger.

Weather_62.explanation: Cumulus congestus and cumulonimbus clouds frequently produce hail aloft, but the hail does not always reach the ground — small hailstones may melt in warmer layers below. The other statements (hail as large as a fist, evaporative downdrafts, 30 m/s gusts) are all true for big storm cells.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: thunderstorm clouds can generate hail and very strong gusts — but hail at ground is not guaranteed, while the gust front is.

Weather_64.explanation: Higher pressure at the surface develops over an area that stays colder than its surroundings for a long time — the dense, cool air piles up. Classic examples are the Siberian or North-Atlantic anticyclones in winter. Warmth, intense sunshine or simply being dry produce the opposite tendency.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: an area that has been colder for longer than its surroundings produces higher air pressure.

Weather_65.explanation: Lower surface pressure develops over an area that stays warmer than its surroundings for a long time — the warm, less dense air rises and "thins" the column. Classic examples: the equator, the summer Sahara, the warm winter ocean compared with cold continents.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: an area that has been warm for longer than its surroundings has lower air pressure — the rising warm air thins the column.

Weather_66.explanation: An extensive low-pressure system at European latitudes typically reads around 955 hPa at its centre — pronounced but not record-breaking. 1035 hPa is a strong high, 915 hPa would be a record-deep cyclone, and 1055 hPa is essentially unheard of in extra-tropical Europe.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a pronounced low in Europe sits around 955 hPa; very strong highs reach 1035 hPa.

Weather_67.explanation: A typical pronounced European anticyclone tops out around 1035 hPa at sea level; in winter, with cold dense air, it can reach 1040 hPa. 1055 hPa is essentially unheard of, while 915 and 955 hPa describe deep lows, not highs.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a strongly developed European high is around 1035 hPa — climate change is now pushing some to 1048 hPa.

Weather_68.explanation: In a northern-hemisphere low (cyclone), surface air spirals anticlockwise around the centre, converges and rises over a large area — this large-scale lift is what produces the cloud and precipitation associated with lows. Clockwise belongs to highs in this hemisphere, and lows don't produce sinking air at their centres.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in the northern hemisphere a high rotates clockwise, a low rotates counterclockwise — and air rises in the low.

Weather_69.explanation: In a northern-hemisphere high (anticyclone), surface air spirals clockwise around the centre, sinks over a large area and warms by compression — this subsidence is what gives anticyclones their famously clear, dry weather. Anticlockwise belongs to lows in this hemisphere, and rising air belongs to lows.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: high pressure = clockwise rotation in the northern hemisphere = positive sunny flying weather, with air sinking.

Weather_70.explanation: An isobar is a contour line on a surface chart connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure, reduced to mean sea level so that high and low altitudes can be compared on the same map. Iso = equal, bar = pressure; not equal temperature, not constant 500 hPa, not the steepest gradient.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: isobar = line of equal pressure, reduced to sea level — iso means equal, bar means pressure.

Weather_71.explanation: From the spacing and orientation of isobars on a surface chart you can read off the prevailing wind strength (close spacing = strong wind) and the prevailing wind direction (parallel to isobars, clockwise around highs / anticlockwise around lows). Temperature gradient or chance of precipitation are not directly encoded in the isobar pattern alone.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: from the isobars we read wind direction parallel to the lines and wind strength from how close together they are.

Weather_72.explanation: Surface winds in flat terrain blow roughly parallel to the isobars, flowing from the centre of a high toward the centre of a low (the air mass is equalising pressure, but the Coriolis force deflects it so it travels along the isobars rather than straight across them). A direct path or the opposite direction would ignore the Coriolis effect.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: air does not flow straight from high to low — the Coriolis force deflects it into a spiral, parallel to the isobars.

Weather_73.explanation: When isobars are squeezed close together the pressure gradient is steep (a lot of hPa change over little distance) — and that drives a strong wind, exactly as a steep slope makes water flow fast. Far-apart isobars = weak gradient = light wind.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the closer the isobars are together, the stronger the wind — and the further apart, the weaker.

Weather_74.explanation: When isobars are spread far apart, the pressure gradient is flat (very few hPa per hundred kilometres) and the surface wind is light — there is little "push" to equalise the pressure. Steep gradients with strong winds need the lines packed tightly together.

guide_chapter.title: Action Centers, Isobars and Prevailing Winds

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the isobars are spread out, the pressure gradient is flat and the wind is weak — think of a topographic map with widely-spaced contours.

Weather_75.explanation: Thermal updrafts develop best in an area of flat pressure gradient (light winds — no mixing-out of the surface bubble) without subsidence inversion (so the rising warm air is not blocked aloft). High-pressure centres usually have a subsidence inversion that caps thermals, low-pressure centres bring weather, and strong gradients mean strong wind, not steady thermals.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the barometric plateau — flat gradient, weak wind, often-good thermals with high ceiling.

Weather_76.explanation: Winds are named by the direction they come FROM, not where they head to. 135° on the compass rose is the southeast quadrant (between 90° = east and 180° = south), so a wind from 135° is a southeast wind. Northeast is 045°, northwest 315°, southwest 225°.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a wind is named for where it comes from — picture the compass rose, 135° comes from the southeast.

Weather_77.explanation: A northwest wind comes from 315° on the compass rose — halfway between north (000°/360°) and west (270°). 045° would be northeast, 135° southeast and 225° southwest.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: north 0°, east 90°, south 180°, west 270° — so northwest is 315°.

Weather_78.explanation: Conversion shortcut: knots × 2 − 10% ≈ km/h. So 25 kt × 2 = 50 km/h, minus 10% (5 km/h) = 45 km/h. That is also why the popular Beaufort cut-offs (15 kt ≈ 27 km/h, 20 kt ≈ 36 km/h) come out so clean.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the simple formula — knots times 2, minus 10%, gives km/h. So 25 kt is 45 km/h.

Weather_79.explanation: 270° on the compass rose is due west, so a wind from 270° is a west wind. Speed: 10 kt × 2 − 10% = 20 − 2 = 18 km/h. East would be 090°, and a 10 kt wind is 18 km/h not 10 km/h.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: 270° comes from the west, and 10 kt times 2 minus 10% gives 18 km/h.

Weather_82.explanation: In flat terrain the surface wind is weaker than the wind at 300 m AGL (ground friction slows it) and at the same time more turbulent (the same friction breaks the flow into eddies). Aloft the wind is faster and smoother — the classic wind gradient profile.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: near the ground the wind is weaker but more turbulent because of friction; aloft it is stronger and more laminar.

Weather_83.explanation: Conversely, at 300 m AGL in flat terrain the wind is stronger (no friction with the ground) and more laminar (less mechanical mixing) than at the surface. This is the wind gradient pilots feel on every flight: smoother and quicker as you climb away from the ground.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in higher air layers the wind is stronger and more laminar — the classic wind gradient.

Weather_84.explanation: The mountain wind is a nighttime katabatic flow: cold air drains from the rapidly-cooling peaks and side valleys down into the main valley. It is the night-and-down-the-valley component of the alpine wind cycle, the opposite of the daytime upslope valley wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at night the mountains cool faster than the lowlands — cold air drains down the valley, that is the mountain wind.

Weather_86.explanation: Under a developed valley wind, thermals concentrate in the upper area of the valley sides — the slopes facing the sun heat strongly, lift slope thermals, and the rising warm air feeds the wind that climbs up the valley. The valley centre is mostly downflow/return air, not lift.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: each sunny slope contributes a thin layer of ascending air on its surface — that is where the lift is during valley wind.

Weather_87.explanation: When the mountain wind takes over at night, the cold drainage flow runs down the valley slopes and the only remaining lift is the slightly warmer air in the middle of the valley being pushed upward — restitution lift. That is why nighttime thermals are usually centred on the valley axis, not on the cold slopes.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at night, as cold air drains down, the warmer air in the middle of the valley sometimes rises — the restitution thermal.

Weather_88.explanation: The valley wind exists because mountains heat more efficiently than the flatlands during the day: the slopes warm faster and harder, lowering the pressure over the peaks. Air flows up the valley from the cooler flatlands to equalise — the daytime upslope component of the cycle.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the alpine slopes heat faster than the lowlands, lowering pressure over the mountains and pulling the valley wind up from the flatlands.

Weather_89.explanation: Mountain (katabatic) wind sets in because mountain slopes lose heat by radiation faster than the broad valley floor when the sun weakens — the cold, dense air on the slopes runs downhill into the warmer main valley. The opposite happens during the day (slopes warm faster), driving the valley wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at night the mountains cool faster than the lowlands — that pressure difference drives the mountain wind down the valley.

Weather_90.explanation: In summer in the Alps, the valley wind starts to set in around 11:00 once the sun has warmed the slopes enough to drive the upslope flow; it peaks mid-afternoon around 15:00. At 08:00 the system is too cold and at 18:00–22:00 it has already reversed into the mountain wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind kicks in around 11, peaks around 3 in the afternoon — and the mountain wind takes over around 6 pm.

Weather_91.explanation: In summer in the Alps, the mountain wind starts to set in around 18:00 — the sun is fading, the high slopes cool fastest and the cold drainage begins. By 22:00 it is fully established and by 11:00 the next morning it has reversed back to the valley wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the mountain wind usually starts around 6 pm as the mountains cool faster than the lowlands.

Weather_92.explanation: The alpine valley-wind system is driven by solar heating of the slopes, so it is strongest in the summer half-year (June–August) when the sun is high and the inner Alps are snow-free. December–January, with low sun, snow cover and weak heating, sees the weakest valley winds.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind needs thermal activity to kick in — it is strongest in summer, almost absent in winter.

Weather_93.explanation: The valley wind increases with decreasing altitude — it is strongest right on the valley floor where the cross-section is narrowed and the flow concentrates, and it gradually fades with height, typically disappearing around 2000 m where the prevailing wind takes over. At 800 m above ground it is already much weaker than at 50 m.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind is really in the valley floor — at 500 m above ground, depending on the valley, it is already much weaker.

Weather_94.explanation: A late-summer afternoon (30 August, 15:00) with 3/8 to 5/8 cumulus is the textbook valley-wind situation: good solar heating, plenty of thermal activity, sun-driven slope heating — all the ingredients are present. Cloudless December at 14:00 is too weak, overcast or 20:00 in June miss the heating, and so on.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind needs thermal activity, sun and good heating — summer afternoons under broken cumulus are perfect.

Weather_95.explanation: Mechanical turbulence is generated when air moving close to the ground is forced around solid obstacles — trees, houses, ridges, mountains. The flow breaks into eddies on the leeward side of each obstacle, which is why landing zones behind buildings or behind a ridge get bumpy.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: mechanical turbulence is when wind flows over a house, a forest or a mountain and creates eddies behind it.

Weather_96.explanation: Shear turbulence occurs at the boundary between two air masses moving in different directions or at different speeds: they rub against each other and the friction breaks the flow into eddies. Mechanical turbulence is obstacle-driven, thermal turbulence is buoyancy-driven, and "shear layers from an obstacle" is mostly mechanical.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when low-level Bise meets upper west wind, the two layers rub and shear turbulence develops where they meet.

Weather_97.explanation: Thermal turbulence is generated when warm air bubbles release from the ground and rise — the climbing column is surrounded by sink at its edges, creating the characteristic bumpy edge of every thermal. Mechanical and shear turbulence have different origins (obstacles, wind shear).

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: thermal turbulence happens because the rising thermal is surrounded by small downdraft zones — wind shear in the vertical.

Weather_98.explanation: At 500 m above ground in the middle of a valley, behind a passed cold front with strong NW wind aloft and a 25 km/h valley wind below, the bumps come from wind shear between the two different flows meeting in mid-valley. This is shear (not mechanical or thermal) turbulence.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when valley wind blows under a different prevailing wind above, the meeting layer is full of shear turbulence.

Weather_100.explanation: On a summer afternoon at 13:00, a sun-baked south-facing slope at 2800 m AGL is a thermal factory — the rising columns of warm air over the slope are the dominant turbulence source there. Mechanical and shear effects are present but much weaker than the buoyant thermal activity at that time and place.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: above a sunny south-facing slope at altitude on a summer day, the dominant turbulence is thermal — the rising bubbles.

Weather_101.explanation: In mid-summer in an alpine valley, the calmest, smoothest air is in the early morning around 09:00 — the night inversion is still in place, the valley wind has not started, and thermals have barely begun. By 11:00 thermals kick in, 15:00 is peak turbulence, and 18:00 brings the mountain wind transition.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind starts around 11, peaks around 3 — so 9 am is the calmest moment.

Weather_102.explanation: In an alpine valley in summer, turbulence peaks in mid-afternoon around 15:00 — that is when thermals are strongest, the valley wind is at its peak (often 30–60 km/h on the floor) and shear plus mechanical effects pile on top. 09:00 is still calm, 12:00 building, 19:00 already calming as the mountain wind sets in.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: peak valley wind is around 3 pm — that is also peak turbulence in the alpine valleys in summer.

Weather_103.explanation: Around 11:00 the day is heating up but the strong valley wind has not yet fully kicked in — thermals are present but mechanical and shear turbulence (from the still-weak valley flow) are minimal. By 15:00 the valley wind is hammering and shear/mechanical effects dominate the bumpiness; 09:00 has no thermals yet; 18:00 the wind has shifted.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind only really starts firing around 11 — so that is when thermals dominate before the wind shear builds in the afternoon.

Weather_104.explanation: In meteorology an "air mass" is a body of air with uniform characteristics — temperature, humidity, pressure — over a large horizontal area (often continental-scale). Mass per cubic metre at a specific reference, density-uniform air, or a concentration centre describe other concepts.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: an air mass is simply a volume of air with the same properties spanning hundreds of kilometres — same temperature, same humidity.

Weather_105.explanation: For frontal analysis, an air mass is "warm" only relative to the adjacent air mass it borders — temperatures are always compared between masses, not against any absolute number. A −10°C polar air mass can be the "warm" one if its neighbour sits at −20°C.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: air masses are always relative — one is colder, one is warmer; even at minus 20 vs minus 10, the −10 is the "warm" one.

Weather_106.explanation: A front is the boundary surface between two different air masses — typically with very different temperatures and humidities. It is a three-dimensional, sloped surface (not just a line at sea level), and is the geometric/thermodynamic divide rather than a precipitation zone.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in between two different air masses there is an air-mass boundary — that is described by a front.

Weather_107.explanation: Because the warm air slopes up and over the cold air ahead, a warm front reaches a ground observer first as high cloud (cirrus, then thickening layers) before the surface front arrives — it is felt at altitude first, then descends to ground level. A cold front is the opposite: cold air slides under at the surface first, then the warm air aloft retreats.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the warm front comes in in a wedge shape — first the high clouds, then the front slowly slopes down to the ground.

Weather_109.explanation: A warm front pushes warm humid air up over the retreating cold air, so its cloud sequence shows the slow approach: high cirrus first, then cirrostratus, then thickening altostratus, finally low rain-producing nimbostratus. The other sequences mix cumulus-family or cold-front clouds and do not match the warm-front geometry.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a warm front brings cirrostratus, then altostratus, then nimbostratus — always tied to stratus clouds.

Weather_110.explanation: The hour before a warm front arrives at the ground typically brings poor visibility, a low cloud base, steady rain or drizzle from the dense nimbostratus deck, and a steady increasing wind — not gusty cumulus weather and not a clear, calm break. The other options describe pre-cold-front or general fair-weather conditions.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: just before a warm front arrives, the cloud base is low, visibility is poor and rain is steady — definitely not flying weather.

Weather_111.explanation: South Föhn on the north side of the Alps announces itself with mountain-clinging clouds (the Föhn wall) in the distance to the south, cumulus and lenticular clouds in the otherwise blue sky, a freshening southwest wind at launch and shifty ground-wind directions. Steady west wind with cumulonimbus distant, blue sky with widespread lift, or solid rain are all different patterns.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Air Active instructor: read off the Föhn from the temperature difference between Ticino and the north side — and even a 4 hPa difference puts Föhn fully in motion.

Weather_112.explanation: South Föhn in the alpine valleys is most likely when Switzerland sits in the warm sector of a depression with a cold front approaching the Jura — pressure builds south of the Alps, drops north, and the south-to-north pressure difference drives air over the ridges. Cirrostratus advance, southwest aloft over Bise below or post-cold-front patterns don't produce that specific south-to-north pressure gradient.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: classic Föhn pattern — a cold front approaches from the west, a Föhn knee forms, and the Föhn flow sets up ahead of the front.

Weather_113.explanation: North Föhn in Ticino develops when Switzerland sits behind a cold front and moderately humid, cold polar air piles up against the north side of the Alps — pressure builds north, drops south, and air is forced from north to south over the ridges. The result: rain and clouds on the north side, dry warm wind in Ticino.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in our case Foehn goes south to north, but a North Foehn is the mirror situation — strong wind and dry warm conditions in Ticino, rain on the north side.

Weather_114.explanation: Föhn requires a strong pressure difference between the north and south sides of the Alps — typically more than 4 hPa across Zurich-Lugano. The greater the difference, the more violently air is forced over the ridges. A small difference cannot drive Föhn, and a high or low centred directly over the Alps eliminates the cross-Alps gradient entirely.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Air Active instructor: 4 hPa is the practical cut-off for Föhn — from 4 hPa upward it really becomes noticeably strong, so I never go above.

Weather_116.explanation: An occlusion forms when the faster cold front catches up with the slower warm front at ground level and pinches the warm sector aloft. Mechanically it is always the cold front at ground level overtaking the warm front there — high-altitude overtaking or the reverse pattern is not what defines an occlusion.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the cold front is roughly twice as fast as the warm front, catches up at ground level, and occlusions form.

Weather_117.explanation: An occluded front carries the weather characteristics of either a warm front or a cold front depending on whether the cold air behind the cold front is warmer or colder than the cold air ahead of the warm front — that comparison sets which front is at the surface and therefore what observers experience.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: occlusion with cold-front character vs warm-front character — depending on which dividing line touches the ground.

Weather_118.explanation: This sketch shows an occlusion with cold-front character — the cold-front dividing line is the one that reaches the ground, meaning the cold air behind the system is colder than the cold air ahead of it. So at the surface, observers experience cold-front weather (gusts, thunder, abrupt change) while warm-front cloud layers hide aloft.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: which dividing line touches the ground decides the character — cold-front line at ground = cold-front-character occlusion.

Weather_119.explanation: This sketch shows an occlusion with warm-front character — the warm-front dividing line is the one that reaches the ground, meaning the cold air behind the system is warmer than the cold air ahead of it. Surface weather looks like a warm front (steady rain, layered clouds), with cold-front clouds hidden above.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: when the warm-front line touches the ground, the occlusion has warm-front character — surface weather mimics a warm front.

Weather_120.explanation: This cross-section shows the wedge of warm air gently sliding up over the retreating cold air — the slowly-rising slope and high-to-low cloud sequence above are the hallmarks of a classic warm front (cirrus → cirrostratus → altostratus → nimbostratus).

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the warm front comes in as a wedge — the warm air glides up on top of the colder air, far less dangerous than a cold front.

Weather_121.explanation: This cross-section shows the teardrop of cold dense air pushing in under the warm air and shoving it abruptly upward — the steep frontal slope and barrier-style cumulonimbus tower above are the signature of a cold front, not a warm front or an occlusion.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the cold front comes in as a teardrop — cold air pushes in underneath, warm air is forced violently upward and thunderstorms form.

Weather_122.explanation: Frontal cumulonimbus and thunderstorms are most likely with a cold front in August: in summer the lower atmosphere is hot and humid, so the abrupt cold-front uplift of warm moist air triggers the most violent storms. Warm fronts in any season have gentle uplift, and January cold fronts lack the warm sector's heat and humidity.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a really active cold front always brings thunderstorms — especially when the lower atmosphere is hot and humid, i.e. in summer.

Weather_123.explanation: When hot humid air sits over Switzerland and cold polar air is forecast to push in from the northwest, the textbook outcome is a violent cold-front passage with frontal thunderstorms, hail and strong gusty winds — the rapidly-rising warm humid air feeds powerful cumulonimbus along the front. Continuous gentle cooling or stable post-frontal conditions don't fit this synoptic setup.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a really active cold front always brings thunderstorms, strong wind and gusts — and very dangerous flying conditions.

Weather_124.explanation: Thermal (local) thunderstorms develop late in the afternoon, after hours of solar heating have built deep cumulus towers into mature cumulonimbus. Mornings are far too cool and only just-after-noon is rarely warm enough; "no time-of-day effect" applies only to frontal storms.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: thermal storms need the engine of the sun — they form late in the afternoon when cumulus towers have grown all morning.

Weather_125.explanation: Frontal thunderstorms can occur at any time of day or night — they are driven by the dynamic uplift of warm air at the front, not by solar heating. So time of day has no influence on frontal storms, unlike thermal/local storms which peak in late afternoon.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a really active cold front always brings thunderstorms — and the front moves at 40–70 km/h regardless of time of day.

Weather_126.explanation: A high centred over northern Germany sets up Bise: surface winds wrap clockwise around the high, so on the Swiss plateau the flow comes from the northeast. Northwest wind, west wind or south Föhn require different synoptic setups.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a Bise — northeast wind — comes when a high sits to the north or northeast of Switzerland.

Weather_127.explanation: When the Azores high extends towards central Europe, the clockwise circulation on its northern flank pushes air across the Alps from the northwest. That gives Switzerland a northwest wind — not a westerly, not Bise, and not Föhn.

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: high pressure rotates clockwise in the northern hemisphere — on the eastern flank of the Azores high you get northwest flow over central Europe.

Weather_129.explanation: High-reaching alpine thermals develop best under a weak high-pressure system centred over the Alps — pressure difference is small (so wind stays light), the air column is dry enough to allow strong heating, and the temperature gradient becomes steep enough for deep convection. A strong high suppresses convection, a low over Genoa brings unstable wet weather, and the Azores ridge pushes in wind.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in highs the air sinks, in lows it rises — for great thermals you want the weak high-pressure barometric plateau where the lapse rate is large but winds stay calm.

Weather_130.explanation: A ground-level inversion forms classically when the sky is clear at night: the ground radiates heat away into space, the air in contact with it cools below the temperature of the air a few hundred metres above, and a stable, cold layer sets up. Daytime heating, advection or anticyclonic subsidence don't create the same near-surface temperature reversal.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

diagrams.title: Emagram with inversion and isothermal layers

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: clear nights cool the ground by radiation, and the cold air pool at the surface ends up below warmer air aloft — that is a nocturnal ground inversion.

Weather_131.explanation: The morning sun is what kills a ground-level inversion: once solar radiation starts heating the ground again, the surface air warms, convection kicks in and the cold pool mixes out. Nocturnal radiation, sinking warm air aloft and advection above can't reverse the surface inversion on their own.

guide_chapter.title: Air Temperature, Atmospheric Warming & Temperature Curves

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at sunrise the engine — the sun — starts firing, heats the ground, and the ground-level inversion gradually disappears through the morning.

Weather_132.explanation: The best thermals develop where the sun's rays strike the ground at a high (near-perpendicular) angle and reflection is weak (dark, dry surface like a ploughed field) — so the maximum solar energy goes into heating the soil. Low sun angles or reflective surfaces (snow, water) waste the energy.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the sun's rays hitting a surface at a right angle warm it much more strongly — and a reflective surface like snow basically wastes the energy.

Weather_133.explanation: Good thermals form best over ground that is wind-protected (so the warm surface pocket can build up without being blown away) and dry (no energy wasted on evaporation). A wind-exposed or moist surface lets either the wind or the latent-heat draw of evaporation kill the thermal before it triggers.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a dry harvested field warms much more than a wet green meadow — wet ground loses energy to evaporation.

Weather_134.explanation: In the pre-Alps, thermals peak in May: the snow has just gone, the ground heats fast, the sun is high, and the contrast with still-snowy higher Alps drives strong convection. August thermals are best in the high Alps (snow then gone there too), October/January are too weak.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the valley wind in spring can be very strong — same logic: the pre-Alps lose their snow first, so May is peak thermal season there.

Weather_135.explanation: In the Alps, thermals reach their highest altitudes in August — the inner alpine snowfields are finally gone, the heating is at its strongest, and the temperature gradient through the deep convective layer (often 2–4 km AGL) is steepest. May is the pre-Alps peak; October/January are far too cold and snowy.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: peak thermal activity is in summer once the inner Alps are snow-free — that is August in the high Alps.

Weather_136.explanation: A "lee thermal" forms on the wind-protected (lee) side of a mountain. The wind-sheltered slope lets warm air build up there for longer than on a wind-swept windward face, so the thermal that does release tends to be stronger — but also more turbulent because of the lee rotor.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: a lee thermal is a thermal on the wind-protected side of a mountain — usually stronger, but more turbulent because of mechanical lee rotors.

Weather_138.explanation: A "blue thermal" is convection rising in air so dry that the parcel never reaches its dew point — no condensation, no cumulus marker. The visible clue is a clear sky despite active thermals; pilots have to find lift without the helpful cumulus signposts.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: blue thermals — the temperature advantage is exhausted before condensation can happen, so no cloud marker forms.

Weather_139.explanation: Blue thermals occur specifically when the temperature–dew-point spread of the rising air is so large that the dew point is never reached during the climb — no condensation, no cumulus. Origin from higher layers, cooling water bodies or stable stratification describe other conditions entirely.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: blue thermals exist when the air is dry enough that the rising bubble never reaches its dew point and so no cloud forms.

Weather_140.explanation: "Restitution lift" is the gentle thermal updraft that forms in the middle of the valley after the katabatic mountain wind has set in — the slightly warmer valley-floor air is squeezed upward by the cold drainage flow on either side, producing a soft, often nighttime lift on the valley axis.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: as cold air drains down at night, the warmer air in the middle of the valley sometimes rises — a kind of reverse thermal, a convergence.

Weather_141.explanation: A symbol on a weather map showing the curved line with the regularly-spaced semicircular bumps (no triangles, no purple half/half mix) is the standard meteorological pictogram for a warm front — semicircles point in the direction of frontal advance.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: red bumps — semicircles — describe a warm front, blue triangles describe a cold front.

Weather_142.explanation: A symbol on a weather map showing the curved line with the regularly-spaced triangular spikes is the standard meteorological pictogram for a cold front — triangles point in the direction of frontal advance, mirroring the abrupt teardrop shape of the front.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: blue triangles describe a cold front — the shape of the symbol reflects the steep cold-air wedge.

Weather_143.explanation: The same warm-front pictogram appears again here — curved line with semicircles pointing in the direction of advance. (Multiple identical questions appear on the SHV exam to test that you know the symbol regardless of orientation.)

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: red bumps — semicircles — always describe a warm front, regardless of orientation.

Weather_144.explanation: A symbol combining both semicircles and triangles on the same line — typically purple in colour — is the meteorological pictogram for an occluded front, where the cold front has overtaken the warm front and the two air-mass boundaries have merged.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: occlusion = mixed form of the two fronts; the symbol mixes the semicircles and triangles.

Weather_145.explanation: On Figure M32 the centre of the depression lies just south of Lulea (Sweden), where the isobars circle around a value near 975 hPa — the lowest pressure on the chart. Stockholm and the Bay of Biscay are nowhere near a closed low; Tunis is firmly in high-pressure territory.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: on this chart the strong low is at Lulea, the strong high is at the Bay of Biscay — recognise the closed-rings pattern.

Weather_146.explanation: On Figure M32 the centre of the anticyclone sits over the Bay of Biscay — closed isobars rising to about 1030 hPa surround the centre there. Tunis, Stockholm and Lulea lie at lower pressures or are nearer the low.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the strong high is over the Bay of Biscay at around 1030 hPa — that is where the closed high-pressure rings sit.

Weather_147.explanation: The flattest pressure gradient on Figure M32 is over North Africa — the isobars are very widely spaced near Tunis, indicating little change of pressure with distance and therefore weak winds. Closer-packed lines (Stockholm, around the low) mean steeper gradients.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: where the isobars are well-spaced, the gradient is flat and the wind weak — Tunis on this chart.

Weather_148.explanation: On Figure M32 the steepest pressure gradient — most tightly packed isobars — runs around Stockholm in southern Scandinavia, where the air streams from the high toward the deep low at Lulea. That is where the wind is strongest on the chart.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: close-together isobars near Stockholm mean a steep gradient and strong wind — far apart in the Mediterranean means weak wind.

Weather_149.explanation: On Figure M32 the highest wind speeds are over southern Scandinavia, where the isobars are packed most tightly — strong west-to-northwest flow parallel to the lines as air streams from the Biscay high toward the Lulea low.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: tightly-packed isobars near Stockholm = strong winds, parallel to the lines.

Weather_150.explanation: On Figure M32 the weakest winds are over North Africa: the isobars are widely spaced (flat gradient) and that area also sits near the edge of the anticyclone, where surface flow is slow. Switzerland is in moderate flow, southern Scandinavia is the windy zone.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: very wide isobar spacing in North Africa and the Mediterranean = weak wind there.

Weather_151.explanation: On Figure M32, with isobars arching from the Biscay high around toward the Mediterranean, the wind direction over Algiers reads as a weak westerly, around 280° — parallel to the isobars and following the clockwise circulation around the high to its south.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the wind is read parallel to the isobars — and around a high in the northern hemisphere the circulation is clockwise.

Weather_152.explanation: On Figure M32, London sits between the Biscay high and the Lulea low in tightly packed isobars; the surface wind reads as a fresh northwesterly around 310°, parallel to the isobars as air streams from the high toward the low.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: read the wind parallel to the isobars — close-together lines mean strong wind.

Weather_153.explanation: On Figure M32, Athens sits under the southern flank of an anticyclone over Russia, with sparse isobars; surface wind reads as a weak southeasterly around 130° — parallel to the isobars, following the clockwise flow on the south side of the high.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: pick the isobar passing through the city and read off the wind direction parallel to it.

Weather_154.explanation: On Figure M33 (the south Föhn situation), Zurich sits in the warm sector with isobars running from southwest to northeast over Switzerland; surface wind reads as a fairly strong southwesterly, around 210°. That south-to-west flow is the very gradient that drives Föhn.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: read the wind parallel to the isobars — southwest over Zurich on a Föhn day.

Weather_155.explanation: On Figure M33, Lisbon sits behind the cold-front trailing edge under the Atlantic anticyclone; surface wind reads as a weak northwesterly around 320° — parallel to the widely-spaced isobars on the eastern flank of the high.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at Lisbon, in light pressure gradient, weak northwesterly parallel to the isobars.

Weather_156.explanation: On Figure M33 the warm sector lies between the leading warm front and the trailing cold front and stretches across central and northern Europe. Zurich and Stockholm both sit inside that wedge of warm subtropical air — Paris and London are behind the cold front, Lisbon and Athens are in anticyclonic high-pressure air.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: in a low, a warm front leads and a cold front trails — the warm sector is the wedge between them.

Weather_157.explanation: On Figure M33, Paris and London lie just behind the cold front in the post-frontal "train" — typical cumulus clouds, fresh polar air, good visibility and stratocumulus. Zurich and Stockholm are still in the warm sector, Lisbon and Athens under anticyclonic influence.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: behind a cold front the air is fresher, with cumulus clouds at low altitudes — Paris and London in the train.

Weather_158.explanation: On Figure M33 the warm front lies far to the north; Lulea and St. Petersburg sit ahead of it and feel the approach first — cirrostratus and high cloud streaming in, pressure falling. Paris/London are post-cold-front, Zurich/Stockholm are in the warm sector, Lisbon/Athens are under highs.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: ahead of a warm front you see cirrostratus and altostratus advancing — the high-cloud announcement.

Weather_159.explanation: On Figure M33 Lisbon sits under the Atlantic anticyclone and Athens sits under the Russian anticyclone — both cities are in high-pressure influence with stable, settled weather. Paris/London are post-frontal, Zurich/Stockholm are in the warm sector, Lulea/St. Petersburg are ahead of the warm front.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: at Athens we have high-pressure influence with nice weather; Lisbon is under the Atlantic high.

Weather_160.explanation: At Lulea on Figure M33, hundreds of kilometres ahead of the warm front advancing from the south, the cirrostratus deck is already overhead with 5/8–7/8 cover — the textbook warm-front herald. Cumulus, cumulonimbus or nimbostratus belong to other parts of the frontal cycle.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: warm front cloud sequence — cirrus, then cirrostratus far ahead of the front itself.

Weather_161.explanation: At Paris on Figure M33, behind the cold front in the post-frontal "train", broken cumulus dominates at 3/8–5/8 cover — fresh, cool air with bubbling shallow convection. 8/8 nimbostratus is warm-front, 5/8–7/8 cumulonimbus belongs ahead of the cold front, cirrostratus is far ahead of the warm front.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: behind a cold front the air is fresher with cumulus clouds at low altitudes.

Weather_162.explanation: At northeastern Switzerland on Figure M33, just ahead of an advancing cold front in a warm-sector Föhn pattern, expect light cumulus cover (2/8–4/8) plus lenticular clouds at altitude — strong southwesterly wind over the alpine ridges generates the lee waves. Nimbostratus or cirrostratus belong elsewhere on the chart.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: with strong wind over a mountain range you get the wave clouds — lenticulars to the east.

Weather_163.explanation: At St. Petersburg on Figure M33, very close to (and just under) the advancing warm front, the warm air rising over the cold air mass is producing a fully-developed nimbostratus deck — 8/8 cover with steady rain. Cumulus belongs post-front, cumulonimbus belongs ahead of the cold front.

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: nimbostratus with showers is the wrap-up of the warm-front sequence as it arrives at the ground.

Weather_164.explanation: Figure M33 is a south-Föhn synoptic situation: strong southwesterly flow with cumulonimbus advancing ahead of a cold front from the west, lenticulars in the east, and dry warm conditions over the Alpine valleys due to south Föhn. So the forecast is "increasing cloud in west and south, mainly sunny in the east due to south Föhn".

guide_chapter.title: Some Weather Situations Typical For Switzerland

video_clips.snippet: Fabio: the classic south Föhn pattern — cold front approaching from the west, Föhn knee over the Alps, strong southwest wind.

Aerodynamics.title: Vectors and forces
Aerodynamics.description: Resolve and combine forces with the parallelogram rule and special-case vector addition.

Aerodynamics.title: Drag fundamentals and drag coefficient
Aerodynamics.description: Identify what drives drag on a body and interpret the drag coefficient (Cd) for different shapes.

Aerodynamics.title: Drag scaling with area and speed
Aerodynamics.description: Work out how drag changes when frontal area or airflow speed is scaled up or down.

Aerodynamics.title: Air density and altitude
Aerodynamics.description: Reason about how decreasing air density with altitude changes the drag a body experiences.

Aerodynamics.title: Wing profile and lift generation
Aerodynamics.description: Read airfoil geometry (chord, leading edge, stagnation, separation, centre of pressure) and understand how lift is produced and distributed.

Aerodynamics.title: Angle of attack and stall
Aerodynamics.description: Use the angle of attack to predict lift, speed, and the onset of stall under different loadings.

Aerodynamics.title: Wing geometry and wing loading
Aerodynamics.description: Compute and compare span, average chord, aspect ratio, surface area, and wing loading for a glider.

Aerodynamics.title: Equilibrium and the resultant aerodynamic force
Aerodynamics.description: Balance lift, drag, and weight in stable flight and read force diagrams showing the resultant aerodynamic force.

Aerodynamics.title: Glide ratio and glide distance
Aerodynamics.description: Relate glide ratio to glide angle, distance flown, height lost, forward speed, and sink rate.

Aerodynamics.title: Polar curve and speed selection
Aerodynamics.description: Read the polar curve to find best-glide, minimum-sink, trim, and maximum speeds, and choose the right speed in sink or head wind.

Aerodynamics.title: Axes, stability, and turns
Aerodynamics.description: Name roll, pitch, and yaw axes, classify glider stability, and apply load factor in turning flight.

Materials.title: Sail cloth and coating
Materials.description: Ripstop weave, porosity, the role of the canopy coating, UV damage and protecting fabric from chemicals and abrasion.

Materials.title: Wing construction
Materials.description: Cells, ribs, V-ribs, stitching, leading-edge loads and how the canopy structure carries lift to the lines.

Materials.title: Line materials and construction
Materials.description: Aramid versus Dyneema cores, sheath function, splicing versus sewing, line diameter and tensile strength.

Materials.title: Line trim and tuning effects
Materials.description: How shortening or lengthening A, B, C or D lines changes angle of attack, handling and flight behaviour.

Materials.title: Line care, damage and replacement
Materials.description: Line stretch, shrinkage, overload damage, torn lines, replacement rules and the line check.

Materials.title: Line groups, geometry and load distribution
Materials.description: Naming and attachment of line groups, A and B versus C and D load share, and the effect of total line length on the pendulum.

Materials.title: Risers, speed bar and trimmers
Materials.description: How the foot accelerator and trim tabs change riser lengths, angle of attack and airspeed.

Materials.title: Brake and steering lines
Materials.description: Brake-line length, pulleys, brake travel calibration and trailing-edge effects under brake input.

Materials.title: Harness geometry and feedback
Materials.description: Diagonal bracing, hang-point height, karabiner spacing, recline angle and their effect on swing and pilot feedback.

Materials.title: Harness back protection
Materials.description: Foam versus ram-air airbag protectors, what they cover and the limits of passive impact protection.

Materials.title: Reserve parachute
Materials.description: Reserve canopy material, mounting and handle access, packing intervals, sink-rate certification and connection geometry.

Materials.title: EN certification, wing loading and structural loads
Materials.description: EN test scheme, wing-loading ranges, tensile loading of fabric and lines, and effects of repeated extreme manoeuvres.

Materials.title: Flight instruments
Materials.description: Variometer, altimeter and anemometer operation and mounting, plus how pressure changes affect altitude readings.

Materials.title: Pilot protective gear
Materials.description: Gloves and helmets, what injuries they prevent and which designs offer the best protection.

Legislation.title: Legal framework (OACS/VLK)
Legislation.description: Federal ordinance basics defining hang gliders, register status, airworthiness responsibility, and the legal source of free-flight rules.

Legislation.title: Aircraft identification & markings
Legislation.description: VKZ rules on identification numbers and manufacturer plaques on the wing.

Legislation.title: Insurance, liability & equipment
Legislation.description: Mandatory third-party liability cover, helmet/reserve obligations and who pays when damages exceed the insured sum.

Legislation.title: Brevet, licensing & exam
Legislation.description: Licence categories, validity, minimum age, training/tandem/instructor prerequisites and exam content, sites, complaints.

Legislation.title: Airspace classes & structure
Legislation.description: ICAO class system, Swiss usage of C/D/E/G, CTR and TMA general definitions, radio/clearance and transponder requirements.

Legislation.title: Cloud clearance & VFR visibility
Legislation.description: VFR visibility minima and cloud-distance values for the various airspaces and altitude bands (including 300 m AGL and LSR-Gliders variants).

Legislation.title: Altitude ceilings & pressure references
Legislation.description: Upper limits of class G and E airspace in Jura vs Alps, flight-level conversions, QNH effect on ceilings and the local 600 m AGL peak rule.

Legislation.title: Restricted, danger & glider zones
Legislation.description: LS-R restricted areas, LS-R for Gliders, LS-D danger zones, MA/HX suffixes and military operating hours.

Legislation.title: Traffic rules & right of way
Legislation.description: Priority by manoeuvrability, head-on encounters in free air and on slopes, overtaking, vertical separation and landing-approach precedence.

Legislation.title: Aviation abbreviations
Legislation.description: Pure abbreviation lookups for terms such as ICAO, VFR, IFR, AGL, AMSL, GND, FL, AWY, CTR, TMA, FIZ, RMZ.

Legislation.title: Information sources & publications
Legislation.description: AIP, GLDK/GLDC, NOTAM and DABS as the standard publications used to plan a VFR flight.

Legislation.title: Glider chart reading & scenarios
Legislation.description: Map symbols, airspace markings and 'X on the chart' situations combining altitude, day/time and zone status.

Legislation.title: Operating rules & ground distances
Legislation.description: Distance to aerodromes, runways, gatherings and obstacles, water take-offs, ground-signal yellow cross and the dedicated free-flight radio frequency.

Practical Flying.title: Flight preparation and airspace info
Practical Flying.description: Researching new sites, reading QNH and foehn pressure differences and consulting glider maps, NOTAM, DABS and obstacle maps before flying.

Practical Flying.title: Pilot physiology, cold and altitude
Practical Flying.description: Effects of altitude, oxygen, wind chill and a head cold on the pilot's body and how to mitigate them in flight.

Practical Flying.title: Wildlife and nature awareness
Practical Flying.description: Respecting golden eagles, chamois, ibex, protected zones, alpine huts and the seasons that matter for wildlife.

Practical Flying.title: Take-off site choice and conditions
Practical Flying.description: Selecting a launch spot, judging wind direction and strength, leeward indicators, forest corridors and the 5-point check.

Practical Flying.title: Take-off execution and immediate problems
Practical Flying.description: Launching technique, abort decisions, brake release timing and reacting to line knots or asymmetric tension right after take-off.

Practical Flying.title: Active piloting, turns and wingovers
Practical Flying.description: Damping pendulum motion, brake limits in straight flight and turns, A-riser acceleration, prolonged deep braking and the dynamics of wingovers.

Practical Flying.title: Stalls and collapses
Practical Flying.description: Recognising and recovering from parachutal stalls, one-sided stalls and asymmetric or large collapses at altitude and near the ground.

Practical Flying.title: Big-ears and spiral-dive descents
Practical Flying.description: Entering, holding and exiting big-ears and spiral dives, their behaviour, sink rates and limits.

Practical Flying.title: Thermalling and ridge soaring
Practical Flying.description: Centring thermals, anticipating their position in wind, leaving for cross country and managing turbulence when soaring a ridge.

Practical Flying.title: Glide, wind drift and crab angle
Practical Flying.description: Reaching a goal under head, tail and cross winds, choosing speed for best glide, computing crab angle and predicting ground track.

Practical Flying.title: Traffic, collision avoidance and reserve use
Practical Flying.description: Sharing airspace with deltas and helicopters, wake turbulence and the situations that demand an immediate reserve deployment.

Practical Flying.title: Landing approach pattern and corrections
Practical Flying.description: Standard downwind/base/final pattern, why we fly an approach, height corrections on each leg and headwind/tailwind/calm-air approach angles.

Practical Flying.title: Special-field landings, post-flight and first aid
Practical Flying.description: Slope, water, tree, valley-wind and obstacle-surrounded fields, wind gradient on touchdown, post-landing actions, helicopter rescue conduct and basic first aid.

Weather.title: Atmospheric basics and pressure
Weather.description: Identify atmospheric composition, ICAO standard atmosphere values, and how pressure changes with altitude or air column properties.

Weather.title: Temperature and adiabatic lapse rates
Weather.description: Reason about dry and saturated adiabatic processes, vertical temperature gradients, and what drives heating or cooling of rising and sinking air.

Weather.title: Inversions, isotherms, and stability
Weather.description: Recognise inversions and isotherms from definitions, soundings, and ground-based signs, and explain how they form or dissipate.

Weather.title: Humidity, dew point, and phase changes
Weather.description: Apply definitions of dew point and the change-of-state vocabulary linking condensation, evaporation, and latent heat to cloud formation.

Weather.title: Cloud types and formation
Weather.description: Identify cumulus, stratus, cirrus, and lenticular families by height and appearance, and link specific clouds to weather and thermal cues.

Weather.title: Pressure systems and isobars
Weather.description: Compare highs and lows, interpret isobar spacing for wind strength, and apply Buys-Ballot-style rules for surface flow.

Weather.title: Wind direction, speed, and surface-layer effects
Weather.description: Convert between compass directions, knots, and km/h, and compare surface wind to wind aloft in flat terrain.

Weather.title: Valley and mountain winds in the Alps
Weather.description: Explain the drivers, daily timing, and typical thermal coupling of upslope valley winds and nocturnal mountain winds.

Weather.title: Mechanical, shear, and thermal turbulence
Weather.description: Distinguish the three turbulence types and predict which dominates in alpine valleys at given times of day and altitudes.

Weather.title: Air masses and fronts
Weather.description: Define air masses and fronts, recognise warm-front cloud sequences and occlusions, and anticipate weather around frontal passages.

Weather.title: Foehn winds
Weather.description: Recognise south and north foehn from pressure setups and visible signs on either side of the Alps.

Weather.title: Thunderstorms and convective hazards
Weather.description: Spot dangerous cumulonimbus and precipitation cues and reason about timing of thermal versus frontal thunderstorms.

Weather.title: Thermals and favourable conditions
Weather.description: Identify the surface, stability, and seasonal conditions that produce strong alpine thermals, including blue, lee, and restitution lift.

Weather.title: Synoptic chart reading and forecasting
Weather.description: Decode weather-map symbols, locate pressure centres and gradients, and read off wind, cloud, and air-mass info for European cities.
